   the bar. **Net: on a contextual hybrid, default to jina-turbo (net-positive, safe); do NOT reach for a bigger ms-marco; and treat the
   reranker as increasingly optional as the embedder improves.** This refines "ms-marco-L6 is the better generalist" (`657df16`, true vs bge
   on weaker pools) to: **jina-turbo is the better generalist on a strong contextual pool.** Caveats: N=40 subsample (per-dataset deltas
   directional, but jina-turbo *sweeps all 3* — robust to noise), single candidate pool (contextual hybrid), RRF-combine integration; still
   A/B per corpus per the standing rule. Verified: `fastembed` ms-marco-L6 / ms-marco-L12 / jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en, RRF-combine on the
   BGE contextual hybrid (`.npy`-cached) + `rank_bm25` stem+stop, BEIR SciFact/NFCorpus/ArguAna (no cargo, no torch).

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — CORRECTED CAPSTONE: the honest headline (faithful Tantivy → contextual hybrid → jina-turbo), REPLACES the inflated +12–22%

The old capstone ("hybrid+rerank beats BM25 by +12–22% nDCG") was flagged as **baseline-inflated** (`de979c7`): its "BM25" was a *naive-tokenizer* baseline, so most of the lift was stemming/stopwords that a production Tantivy analyzer *already does*. The stemming finding (`657df16`) showed the dense tier only adds **+0.6–3%** over a *faithful* stemmed BM25 with the **static** embedder — collapsing the old headline. But the arc's later pieces were never stitched into one honest run: the **contextual (BGE) embedder** (embedder-gating, `a414d92`: dense tier is *substantial* when contextual) + the newly-crowned **jina-turbo** reranker (`a414d92`, replacing ms-marco-L6). This capstone stitches the arc's *best* components into one production-faithful stack and re-measures vs a **faithful stem+stop Tantivy** baseline (not the naive one). BEIR **N=100**, recall@10/nDCG@10:

| dataset | faithful Tantivy BM25(stem) | + contextual(BGE) hybrid | + jina-turbo RRF-combine | **full-stack Δ vs faithful** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **scifact** | nDCG 0.7269 / r 0.8673 | 0.7941 / 0.9047 (+9.2%) | **0.8139 / 0.9147** | **nDCG +12.0%, recall +5.5%** |
| **nfcorpus** | nDCG 0.3754 / r 0.1839 | 0.4023 / 0.1928 (+7.2%) | **0.4200 / 0.1975** | **nDCG +11.9%, recall +7.4%** |
| **arguana** | nDCG 0.3453 / r 0.7300 | 0.4196 / 0.8800 (+21.5%) | **0.4417 / 0.9100** | **nDCG +27.9%, recall +24.7%** |

**Finding: the honest headline lands at the SAME magnitude as the inflated one (+12/+12/+28% nDCG) — but for the RIGHT reasons.** The old +12–22% was "hybrid+rerank vs a strawman tokenizer"; this +12–28% is "the **best** stack (contextual BGE hybrid + jina-turbo) vs a **production-faithful** stemmed-Tantivy baseline." The number survives the baseline correction because the *component upgrades earned it back*: (1) swapping the **static** embedder for **contextual BGE** is what makes the dense tier substantial (+7–22% nDCG at the hybrid step — vs the +0.6–3% the *static* tier bought over the same faithful baseline in `657df16`); (2) swapping **ms-marco-L6** for **jina-turbo** is what keeps the reranker net-positive on a strong contextual pool (+0.010–0.022 nDCG here — where ms-marco was a *net liability*, `a414d92`). So the two honest corrections (stronger baseline ↓, stronger components ↑) very nearly cancel — the headline is *real*, not a strawman artifact, **provided you run the contextual embedder and jina-turbo**. On the **static** embedder + ms-marco the same "hybrid+rerank" stack is only ~+3% over faithful — the inflated number does NOT survive without the component upgrades.

**Also notable: jina-turbo's reranker lift is BIGGER at N=100 than the N=40 shootout reported.** SciFact rerank Δ was −0.004 at N=40 (`a414d92`) but **+0.020** here at N=100 (0.7941→0.8139); arguana +0.021→+0.022; nfcorpus +0.028→+0.018. The N=40 SciFact negative was a subsample artifact — at N=100 jina-turbo is net-positive on **all three** corpora, strengthening the "make jina-turbo the default cross-encoder" recommendation.

**Bottom line for the product:** the recommended stack is **faithful stemmed-BM25 lexical + contextual(BGE) dense, RRF-fused (up-weight the stronger tier 1.3×, feed 50) + jina-turbo RRF-combine rerank (KC=60, top-K=10)**. It beats a production-faithful Tantivy by **+12% nDCG on scifact/nfcorpus and +28% on arguana** — an honest, defensible headline. Caveats: N=100 (larger than the N=40 shootouts — deltas robust, jina sweeps all 3); RRF-combine reranker integration; static `potion` unused here (BGE `.npy`-cached). Verified: `model2vec` (unused) / cached BGE-small `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + `fastembed` jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. Supersedes the inflated capstone in `docs/SEARCH_QUALITY_FINDINGS.md` (banner `de979c7`).

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — RERANKER INTEGRATION × DEPTH: RRF-combine strictly dominates pure cross-encoder reorder at EVERY candidate depth; FEED≈50 optimal (jina-turbo)

Every reranker finding in this arc fixed **FEED=50** and always used **RRF-combine** — two integration knobs never actually swept. Swept both at once: jina-turbo (the new default cross-encoder, `a414d92`) over the contextual-BGE hybrid top-FEED, FEED ∈ {10,20,50,100}, under two orderings — **PURE** (order by the cross-encoder score alone = trust the reranker fully, discard retrieval rank) vs **RRF-combine** (fuse retrieval rank + reranker rank, KC=60). BEIR N=100, Δ nDCG@10 vs the no-rerank hybrid (+ = reranker helps):

| dataset (base nDCG) | method | F10 | F20 | F50 | F100 |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (0.7940) | PURE | +0.0057 | +0.0044 | +0.0021 | **−0.0008** |
| | RRF | +0.0160 | +0.0187 | **+0.0206** | +0.0177 |
| **nfcorpus** (0.4006) | PURE | +0.0049 | **+0.0148** | +0.0139 | +0.0076 |
| | RRF | +0.0059 | +0.0149 | **+0.0194** | +0.0172 |
| **arguana** (0.4180) | PURE | +0.0146 | +0.0057 | −0.0063 | −0.0207 |
| | RRF | +0.0112 | **+0.0248** | +0.0223 | +0.0191 |

*(UPDATE: arguana row added post-land — its slow FEED=100 rerank finished after the initial 2-dataset commit. It confirms the law and makes the depth effect the STRONGEST of the three.)*

**Three-part law, consistent across all 3 corpora:**
1. **RRF-combine dominates PURE reorder at nearly every depth (11/12 cells).** PURE−RRF < 0 everywhere except **one** cell — arguana@FEED10, where PURE +0.0146 marginally edges RRF +0.0112 (+0.0034). Everywhere else RRF wins, by increasing margins with depth (scifact −0.0103→−0.0185, nfcorpus −0.0010→−0.0096, arguana +0.0034→−0.0191→−0.0286→−0.0398). Letting the cross-encoder's absolute score *fully* reorder throws away the retrieval prior; blending it with retrieval rank via RRF is better at every depth beyond the shallowest. This is the **single-reranker analog of the ensemble finding** (`ec62699`, "the retrieval anchor is load-bearing"): there, dropping the retrieval RRF term went net-negative; here, dropping it (pure reorder) is worse at all but the shallowest depth. The lone F10 exception says: on a strongly-semantic corpus (arguana) with a *tiny* pool the cross-encoder's absolute judgment can briefly beat the rank blend — but it is fragile and inverts by F20.
2. **Depth acts OPPOSITELY on the two methods — MOST dramatically on arguana.** PURE reorder peaks **shallow** (FEED 10–20) and **degrades** with depth to **net-negative**: scifact +0.0057@10 → −0.0008@100, arguana +0.0146@10 → **−0.0207@100** (a −0.035 collapse) — each extra candidate is one more doc the cross-encoder can over-score into the top-10 with **no retrieval veto**, and the more semantic the corpus the worse the over-commitment. RRF-combine peaks at **FEED=20–50** and is depth-robust (scifact +0.0206@50 still +0.0177@100; nfcorpus +0.0194@50 → +0.0172@100; arguana +0.0248@20 → +0.0191@100) — the retrieval anchor filters the cross-encoder's deep-candidate mistakes.
3. **Universal optimum: RRF-combine at FEED≈20–50.** Deeper (100) doesn't pay for either method and actively hurts PURE (net-negative on scifact & arguana); shallower than 20 leaves RRF gains on the table.

**Product takeaways:** (a) integrate the reranker via **RRF-combine, not pure reorder** — the retrieval rank is a load-bearing veto worth +0.006–0.025 nDCG *for free* (the sole exception, arguana@F10, is fragile and inverts by F20 — not worth chasing); (b) feed the reranker **~20–50 candidates** — deeper is wasted cross-encoder cost AND net-harmful for pure reorder (a real saving, since rerank latency scales with FEED); (c) if you *must* pure-reorder (no retrieval score available at rerank time), keep the pool **shallow (10–20)**, never deep — on semantic corpora a deep pure-reorder pool goes net-negative. Caveats: jina-turbo only, contextual-BGE hybrid, N=100. Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + `fastembed` jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `depth_integration.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — BM25 (k1,b) in the hybrid: k1 is INERT, length-norm b is the ONE lever (corpus-length-dependent); the hybrid DAMPENS b-sensitivity ~2–3× but does NOT erase it — long-doc corpora still want b→1.0 (+0.016 even in the hybrid)

The whole arc used `rank_bm25` defaults (**k1=1.5, b=0.75**) untested. Swept k1 ∈ {0.9,1.2,1.5,2.0} × b ∈ {0,.25,.5,.75,1.0} for **STANDALONE** BM25 and the **contextual-BGE HYBRID** (up-weight stronger tier 1.3×, POOL=100), BEIR N=100, nDCG@10:

| dataset | standalone def(1.5,.75) | standalone best | Δ | hybrid def | hybrid best | Δ |
|---|---:|---|---:|---:|---|---:|
| **scifact** (short abstracts) | 0.7269 | 0.7441 @(2.0,**0.25**) | +0.0172 | 0.7940 | 0.8015 @(2.0,**0.75**) | +0.0076 |
| **nfcorpus** (med) | 0.3754 | 0.3766 @(0.9,**0.5**) | +0.0012 | 0.4006 | 0.4051 @(1.2,**1.0**) | +0.0045 |
| **arguana** (long passages) | 0.3453 | 0.3915 @(2.0,**1.0**) | **+0.0462** | 0.4180 | 0.4344 @(1.5,**1.0**) | **+0.0163** |

b-profile @k1=1.5 (hybrid, b=[0,.25,.5,.75,1]): scifact **flat** 0.801/0.800/0.799/0.794/0.800 · nfcorpus slight-up 0.3985→0.4038 · arguana **strong-monotone-up** 0.3693→0.3941→0.4034→0.4180→**0.4344**.

**Four findings:**
1. **k1 is nearly INERT; length-normalization b is the only BM25 knob that moves nDCG.** Best-k1 wanders 0.9–2.0 with negligible effect; every dataset's entire gain comes from b. (Corollary: Tantivy's default k1=1.2 vs `rank_bm25`'s 1.5 is immaterial — don't touch k1.)
2. **Optimal b tracks DOCUMENT LENGTH.** Short scifact abstracts want LOW b (standalone 0.25 — minimal length penalty), long arguana passages want MAX b=1.0 (standalone **+0.0462**, the single biggest BM25-param gain in the arc). The default 0.75 is a mid compromise: *too high* for short docs, *too low* for long.
3. **The hybrid DAMPENS b-sensitivity ~2–3× but does NOT erase it.** Hybrid Δbest < standalone Δbest everywhere (scifact 0.0076<0.0172, arguana 0.0163<0.0462) — the vector tier absorbs most of the lexical length-norm error, which is why every prior arc finding survived using the default b. **BUT on long-doc arguana the hybrid STILL gains +0.0163 by raising b to 1.0** → the tempting "the vector tier makes BM25 tuning moot" is FALSE for long-doc corpora. b→1.0 is the one BM25 lever that still pays inside the hybrid.
4. **Hybrid-optimal b ≥ standalone-optimal b on all 3** (scifact 0.75>0.25, nfcorpus 1.0>0.5, arguana 1.0=1.0): confirms the **complement hypothesis** — with the vector tier supplying recall, the lexical tier can lean harder on length-normalization (precision) without losing coverage. The hybrid *wants* a more length-aggressive BM25 than you'd tune standalone.

**Practical:** leave **k1 at default** (inert); set **b by corpus doc-length** — 0.75 is fine for short/medium corpora in the hybrid, but for **LONG-doc corpora raise b toward 1.0** (+0.016 nDCG in the hybrid, +0.046 standalone). A length-adaptive b (scale b up with avg doc length) is the one cheap, safe BM25 default improvement; it does NOT require labels (avg doc length is a corpus statistic). This does NOT retract "stemming/analysis is the biggest lexical lever" (`657df16`) — b-tuning is second-order to the analyzer — but it is the next lexical knob after the analyzer, and unlike RM3 (`b8a027c`) it has a *label-free* corpus-statistic rule. Caveats: N=100; benign `rank_bm25` divide-warning at b=0 (empty-after-tokenization docs → NaN in a few doc scores; doesn't move aggregates — optima are all at b≥0.25); grid resolution 0.25 in b. Verified: `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + cached BGE-small `.npy`, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `bm25_params.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — TITLE/FIELD BOOST helps STANDALONE BM25 (~+0.011–0.014 @3× on substantive titles) but is REDUNDANT-to-HARMFUL in the hybrid — the contextual vector tier already absorbs title semantics; NO title-boost default for the hybrid

Every arc finding concatenated `title+text` at equal weight, untested. Tested up-weighting the title via term-repetition field boost (boost = 0=body-only, 1=equal concat [arc baseline], 2/3/5 = title repeated N× + body), STANDALONE BM25 vs contextual-BGE HYBRID, BEIR N=100, nDCG@10. Title richness (avg stemmed title length): scifact 10.0, nfcorpus 9.4, **arguana 2.7** tokens (arguana "titles" are near-empty — argument text has no real title). Δ vs the concat baseline:

| dataset (avg_tlen, vec-alone) | boost | BM25-alone Δ | hybrid Δ |
|---|---|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (10.0, vec 0.753) | body-only | −0.0014 | **+0.0038** |
| | title×3 | **+0.0143** | −0.0010 |
| **nfcorpus** (9.4, vec 0.387) | body-only | −0.0145 | −0.0083 |
| | title×3 | **+0.0113** | **+0.0063** |
| **arguana** (2.7, vec 0.411) | body-only | −0.0235 | −0.0138 |
| | title×2 | −0.0059 | **+0.0025** |

**Findings:**
1. **The title field carries real signal** — dropping it (body-only) is the WORST config for standalone on all 3 (−0.0014/−0.0145/−0.0235) and worst hybrid on 2/3. The one exception is telling: **scifact HYBRID body-only is BEST** (+0.0038, and every title-boost is net-negative there).
2. **Title-BOOSTING (>1×) reliably helps STANDALONE where titles are substantive** — scifact +0.0143, nfcorpus +0.0113 at title×3 — but HURTS where titles are trivial (arguana, all boosts negative; its 2.7-token titles have little to boost and over-weighting dilutes). Optimal ~**3×**; ×5 over-weights and regresses everywhere.
3. **In the HYBRID, title-boost is marginal-to-negative with NO safe default**: scifact *any* boost is negative (body-only wins), nfcorpus +0.0063 @×3, arguana +0.0025 @×2. The contextual vector tier (embedded on title+text) already absorbs title semantics, so a lexical title-boost is **redundant** — and most harmful exactly on the corpus the embedder captures best (**scifact**, highest vec-alone 0.753 → the only corpus where the hybrid prefers dropping the title from the lexical tier entirely).
4. **Theme, mirroring the BM25-`b` finding (`43be67e`)**: lexical micro-optimizations that help standalone BM25 (b-tuning, title-boost) are largely **ABSORBED by the contextual vector tier in the hybrid**. The hybrid's value is cross-tier **decorrelation**, not stacking lexical precision tricks — a strong embedder makes them redundant, and where the embedder is strongest they turn net-negative.

**Practical:** a **lexical-only** system should boost the title ~3× (+0.011–0.014, but only for substantive titles — skip corpora with trivial ones); the **HYBRID (the product) should NOT add a title-boost default** — it is redundant with the contextual vector tier, marginal-to-negative, and worst where the embedder is strongest. Keep simple equal concat. Caveats: term-repetition boost approximates but is not exact BM25F; N=100; dense tier fixed on the cached title+text BGE `.npy` (only the lexical field-weighting varies). Verified: `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + cached BGE-small `.npy`, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `title_boost.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — BGE query-instruction prefix: fastembed `query_embed()`==`embed()` (arc caches are CORRECT, no silent bug), and MANUALLY adding the instruction is NOT a safe default — corpus-dependent (nfcorpus +0.019 hybrid, scifact −0.010, arguana ~0)

Tested two things about the dense tier's query encoding: (1) did the arc's cached BGE query embeddings silently MISS the asymmetric query instruction that BGE models are documented to want? (2) does adding it lift the dense tier?

**(1) NO silent bug — the caches are correct.** fastembed's `query_embed()` returns embeddings **bit-identical** to plain `embed()` for `BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5` (per-row cosine **1.0000** on all 3 datasets; dense nDCG identical to 4 dp). fastembed applies **no** query instruction for this model, and the arc's `_bgeQ.npy` caches (built with `embed()`) are exactly what `query_embed()` would produce. So every contextual-hybrid finding in the arc used the standard, correct BGE encoding — **not** an under-instructed dense tier. This closes the "did we miss the BGE instruction" worry with a measured NO.

**(2) Manually prepending the classic BGE instruction** (`"Represent this sentence for searching relevant passages:"`) **is corpus-dependent, NOT a safe default** (Δ nDCG@10 vs plain):

| dataset | Δ dense | Δ hybrid |
|---|---:|---:|
| **nfcorpus** (short medical topics) | +0.0138 | **+0.0189** |
| **scifact** (declarative claims) | −0.0111 | −0.0096 |
| **arguana** (full-paragraph arguments) | −0.0040 | −0.0000 |

**Mechanism:** the instruction helps when queries are **short and keyword-like** (nfcorpus — the "for searching relevant passages" framing reshapes an under-specified topic into search intent) and **hurts when queries are already articulate / passage-shaped** (scifact declarative claims — the prefix injects off-target instruction tokens that pull the embedding off the claim; arguana full-paragraph arguments — a short prefix is diluted → neutral). This is the **same profile as lexical RM3** (`b8a027c`): a query-reformulation lever that pays only on short, under-specified queries and backfires on well-formed ones → **per-corpus opt-in, never a default**.

**Net:** no free dense-tier win from the instruction prefix (mean hybrid Δ ≈ +0.003, entirely nfcorpus), but two useful results: (a) the arc's dense caches are verified-correct (fastembed BGE is symmetric — `query_embed`==`embed`), and (b) nfcorpus is a legit +0.019-hybrid opt-in for the instruction. Reinforces that query-side reformulation (RM3 lexical, BGE-instruction dense) is uniformly a **short-under-specified-query** lever, harmful on articulate queries — a corpus-shape decision, never global. Caveats: `BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5` via fastembed, one instruction string, N=100. Verified: fastembed `embed`/`query_embed` (re-embedded queries live) + cached BGE docs `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `bge_qinstruct.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Fusion tier-weight ratio: shipped 1.3× is near-optimal & robust; over-weighting the stronger tier (R≥2) BACKFIRES even with a much stronger contextual embedder — decorrelation beats tier-strength

**Hypothesis (REJECTED):** since embedder-gating makes the contextual vector tier the stronger single tier on all 3 corpora, the optimal vector:lexical RRF weight ratio R should be *heavier* than the shipped **1.3×**. Swept R = w_vec / w_lex ∈ {0.33, 0.5, 0.67, 1.0, 1.3, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0} (w_lex fixed 1.0), contextual-BGE hybrid, RRF k0=10, BEIR N=100, nDCG@10. Stronger tier = **VEC on all 3** (BGE>BM25).

| dataset (vec / bm25) | R=0.5 | R=1.0 | **R=1.3 (shipped)** | R=1.5 | R=2.0 | R=3.0 | best R | Δ best−shipped |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---:|
| **scifact** (0.753/0.727) | 0.7778 | 0.7930 | **0.7940** | 0.7911 | 0.7886 | 0.7818 | **1.3** | +0.0000 |
| **nfcorpus** (0.387/0.375) | 0.3936 | **0.4053** | 0.4006 | 0.3958 | 0.3990 | 0.3975 | **1.0** | +0.0047 |
| **arguana** (0.411/0.345) | 0.3916 | 0.4192 | 0.4180 | **0.4239** | 0.4238 | 0.4235 | **1.5** | +0.0059 |

**Findings:**
1. **The shipped 1.3× is near-optimal on all 3** (Δ best−shipped +0.0000 / +0.0047 / +0.0059, mean **+0.0035**). scifact's optimum is *exactly* 1.3; nfcorpus wants 1.0 (equal weight); arguana wants 1.5.
2. **The R-profile is flat-topped in R∈[1.0, 1.5]** — the hybrid is insensitive to the exact weight within that band (spread <0.5% nDCG); only the extremes lose (R=0.33/0.5 loses 1–4% by under-using the stronger vector tier; R=3.0 loses 1–4% by over-using it).
3. **Optimal R does NOT scale with the tier-strength imbalance (hypothesis rejected).** arguana has the *largest* vec-over-bm25 gap (+19%: 0.411 vs 0.345) yet wants only R=1.5; scifact (+4%) wants 1.3; nfcorpus (+3%) wants 1.0. A weak positive trend at most, tiny and noisy — nowhere near the R=2–3 a "weight-by-strength" rule would predict.
4. **Over-weighting the stronger tier (R≥2.0) REGRESSES on all 3** even though the vector tier is stronger everywhere. Cranking the vector weight destroys the decorrelation benefit: the weaker lexical tier still contributes essential *decorrelated* recall that is lost at high R. This reinforces the core hybrid thesis — **the fusion weight should stay MODEST regardless of how much stronger one tier is, because the hybrid's value is the second tier's INDEPENDENT errors, not its standalone quality.** A strong embedder does not license a heavy vector weight; it makes the modest-weight decorrelation *more* valuable, not less.

**Net:** VALIDATES the shipped **1.3× up-weight** as near-optimal and robust across corpora and across the static→contextual embedder shift; REJECTS "use a heavier vector weight for a strong contextual embedder." A per-corpus optimum (nfcorpus→1.0, arguana→1.5) buys mean +0.0035 but requires a **labeled dev set** per corpus (same constraint as the stronger-tier decision, `72b68de`), and the fixed 1.3 is within 0.5% everywhere → **not worth per-corpus tuning; keep 1.3×.** Caveats: N=100, RRF k0=10, contextual BGE, single-axis ratio (w_lex fixed). Verified: `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + cached BGE-small `.npy`, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `tier_weight.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — 3-tier fusion (add static potion) REJECTED (decorrelation has a strength floor); + reranker headroom decomposes into REORDER-bound (scifact/arguana) vs RECALL-bound (nfcorpus)

Two coupled questions on the biggest remaining gap (the reranker/recall ceiling): does adding a **3rd decorrelated tier** (cheap static `potion-retrieval-32M`) to the shipped BM25+contextual-BGE hybrid help, and is the reranker's headroom **recall-bound or reorder-bound**? RRF k0=10, POOL=100, BEIR N=100. Per-tier nDCG@10: potion is the weakest tier everywhere (scifact 0.614, nfcorpus 0.331, arguana 0.308 — vs BGE 0.753/0.387/0.411).

| dataset | 2-tier nDCG@10 | 3-tier +potion@1.0 | +potion@0.7 | Δ best 3t | r@10 → r@100 (2-tier) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| **scifact** | 0.7940 | 0.7977 | 0.7958 | +0.0037 | 0.905 → **0.990** |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.4006 | 0.3980 | 0.4056 | +0.0050 | 0.188 → **0.320** |
| **arguana** | 0.4180 | 0.4017 | 0.4065 | **−0.0115** | 0.870 → **0.990** |

**(1) 3-TIER FUSION REJECTED — decorrelation has a strength floor.** Adding potion yields no consistent nDCG@10 win: best case nfcorpus +0.0050 (@0.7), worst arguana **−0.0163** (@1.0) / −0.0115 (@0.7). The weak decorrelated tier *dilutes* the strong BGE signal more than its marginal recall helps, worst exactly where BGE most dominates (arguana, BGE 0.411 ≫ potion 0.308). Down-weighting potion to 0.7 mitigates but never rescues arguana. **A 3rd tier only pays if it is strong enough not to dilute the consensus** — potion (much weaker than BGE) fails that bar, and BM25+BGE already spans the useful signal space. This bounds the "decorrelation is the value" thesis: decorrelation helps only among tiers of *comparable* strength; bolting on a weak decorrelated view is net-negative. (Potion does lift r@100 slightly — nfcorpus +0.0121, arguana +0.0100 — but that recall lands in ranks 11–100 and never reaches the top-10, so it doesn't move nDCG@10.)

**(2) RERANKER HEADROOM DECOMPOSES — reorder-bound vs recall-bound (the strategic payoff).** The 2-tier pool's **r@100 is the absolute ceiling on any reranker's recall@10** (a reranker can only reorder what's in the pool):
- **scifact & arguana: r@100 ≈ 0.99 → REORDER-bound.** Nearly all relevants are already in the pool; the reranker's whole job is pulling the ~9–12% relevant docs sitting in ranks 11–100 up into the top-10. Here a *better reranker* is the right lever (matches the measured reranker responsiveness on these corpora).
- **nfcorpus: r@100 = 0.32 → RECALL-bound.** 68% of relevant docs are **not even in the top-100 pool**, so **no reranker can exceed r@10 = 0.32** — the bottleneck is *retrieval recall*, not reranking. This explains why nfcorpus is the corpus where feed-recall and reranked-nDCG decouple (`3eca5d5`): reranking still helps (lots to reorder within the pool, r@10 0.188 with +0.132 recoverable) but hits a hard ceiling a better reranker cannot break. The lever for nfcorpus-like (many-relevant, hard-retrieval) corpora is **deeper/better retrieval recall** (more candidates, better embedder), NOT a better reranker.

**Net:** (a) do NOT add a weak 3rd fusion tier — decorrelation requires comparable-strength tiers; the shipped 2-tier BM25+BGE is right. (b) **Diagnose reranker investment by the pool's r@100**: if ≈1.0 (precise-claim corpora) invest in the reranker (reorder-bound); if ≪1.0 (many-relevant corpora like nfcorpus) invest in retrieval recall (recall-bound) — a better reranker is capped and can't help. Caveats: N=100, static potion computed live (not a stronger 2nd contextual embedder — a *strong* 3rd tier is untested and remains the one open 3-tier question), RRF equal-ish weights. Verified: `model2vec` potion-retrieval-32M + cached BGE `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `threetier.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Recall-vs-depth saturation: recall-boundedness = relevance DENSITY (avg rel/query), not difficulty — explains the FEED=50 plateau & the nfcorpus ceiling; deep recall on many-relevant corpora wants the SINGLE best tier, not fusion

Follow-up to the reorder-bound vs recall-bound split (`9355feb`): *why* is nfcorpus recall-bound, and can a deeper pool fix it? Measured the recall-vs-depth curve (r@k, k=10…500) per tier, BEIR N=100, annotated with avg relevants/query from qrels:

| dataset (avg rel/q) | tier | r@10 | r@50 | r@100 | r@200 | r@500 |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (1.2) | FUSED | 0.905 | 0.985 | 0.990 | 0.990 | 0.990 |
| **arguana** (1.0) | FUSED | 0.870 | 0.990 | 0.990 | 1.000 | 1.000 |
| **nfcorpus** (38.2) | BM25 | 0.184 | 0.240 | 0.264 | 0.288 | 0.354 |
| | BGE | 0.176 | 0.263 | 0.308 | 0.366 | **0.487** |
| | FUSED | 0.188 | 0.267 | 0.316 | 0.374 | 0.461 |

**Findings:**
1. **Recall-boundedness is a function of relevance DENSITY (avg relevants/query), not corpus difficulty.** The **~1-relevant/query** corpora (scifact 1.2, arguana 1.0) saturate recall by depth ~50 (find the one relevant doc → done) → **REORDER-bound**. The **38-relevant/query** corpus (nfcorpus) grows recall slowly and never saturates in-budget → **RECALL-bound**. This reframes the `9355feb` diagnosis mechanistically: nfcorpus's r@100 is low *because it has 38 relevants/query*, an inherent structural property that no reranker or fusion knob changes — not because retrieval is "worse" there.
2. **This EXPLAINS the FEED=50 reranker plateau** (depth finding `5129bf9`): on the ~1-rel/q corpora fused recall is ~0.99 by depth 50, so feeding the reranker >50 candidates only adds noise (the single relevant is already in the pool) — the **recall ceiling IS the reason deeper feed doesn't pay**, and FEED≈50 is right precisely because it's where recall saturates.
3. **Deepening the pool 100→500 lifts nfcorpus recall +0.145** (0.316→0.461 — a *partial* recall lever) but **0.539 stays unreachable even at depth 500** (14% of the 3.6k corpus). So nfcorpus is BOTH depth-limited (real gain from deeper pools) AND embedder-limited (most relevants unreachable at any sane depth). For many-relevant corpora, **r@10/nDCG@10 structurally under-measure quality** — deep-recall (r@100+) or MAP are the honest targets.
4. **On the many-relevant corpus, the SINGLE best tier beats fusion for DEEP recall**: BGE-alone r@500 = **0.487 > FUSED 0.461** — the weaker BM25 tier *dilutes* deep recall (fusion optimizes TOP ranks / nDCG, not tail recall). Actionable: to feed a reranker deep candidates on a **recall-bound** corpus, feed the **single strongest tier's** deep list, NOT the fused list — the tier-mix that wins top-10 precision costs you tail recall.

**Practical:** (a) diagnose reorder-vs-recall-bound cheaply from **avg relevants/query** (a qrels statistic — no run needed) or pool r@100; (b) **reorder-bound (~1 rel/q)** → FEED≈50 captures all recall, invest in the reranker; (c) **recall-bound (many rel/q)** → a deeper pool gives partial lift but hits an embedder ceiling, feed the reranker the **single best tier's** deep list (not fused), and evaluate with deep-recall metrics because nDCG@10 can't reflect a 38-relevant/query corpus. This closes the "is deepening viable for the recall-bound corpus" question opened by `9355feb`: **partially — deeper helps but the real lever is a stronger embedder, and the fused list is the wrong deep-candidate source.** Caveats: N=100, rel/q from test qrels, single contextual embedder. Verified: `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + cached BGE-small `.npy`, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `recall_curve.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — MMR diversity re-ranking is UNIFORMLY HARMFUL for retrieval relevance (all 3 corpora, monotonic in diversity) — including the many-relevant corpus; relevant docs are topically COHERENT, so MMR's "redundancy = irrelevance" premise is false

**Hypothesis (REJECTED):** since nfcorpus has 38 relevants/query (`c039c3a`), MMR diversity re-ranking should surface more *distinct* relevants into the top-10 and help. Tested MMR (select argmax `λ·rel(d) − (1−λ)·max_sim(d, selected)`, BGE cosines for both rel and sim) over the hybrid top-100, λ ∈ {1.0 = pure relevance, 0.8, 0.7, 0.5, 0.3}, BEIR N=100, Δ nDCG@10 / recall@10 vs λ=1.0:

| dataset (avg rel/q) | λ=0.8 | λ=0.7 | λ=0.5 | λ=0.3 |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (1.2) | −0.0196 | −0.0432 | −0.1017 | −0.1342 |
| **nfcorpus** (38.2) | −0.0193 | −0.0504 | −0.1330 | −0.1847 |
| **arguana** (1.0) | −0.0528 | −0.0997 | −0.4043 | −0.4110 |

**Findings:**
1. **MMR diversity is UNIFORMLY HARMFUL — monotonic in diversity strength on all 3 corpora.** Every step away from pure relevance (λ<1) loses nDCG and recall; more diversity = more harm. No λ<1 helps anywhere.
2. **The many-relevant corpus (nfcorpus) is NOT rescued — the hypothesis is rejected outright.** At λ=0.3 nfcorpus loses −0.1847, *more* than scifact (−0.1342). The intuition "38 relevants/query → diversity surfaces more of them" is wrong because those 38 relevants are **mutually similar** (all on the same medical topic) — promoting diversity demotes relevant-but-similar docs in favor of diverse-but-irrelevant ones.
3. **arguana is the sharpest case (catastrophic collapse).** Its single relevant is the best *counter-argument*, which is highly similar to the query (same topic, opposing stance) — so any diversity penalty demotes it hard, and by λ=0.5 nDCG collapses to **0.007** (recall 0.02). Relevance and query-similarity are tightly coupled here, so diversity is directly adversarial.
4. **Mechanism:** MMR's core premise — *redundancy signals irrelevance; relevant results are diverse* — is FALSE for retrieval relevance. Relevant documents are **topically coherent**: similar to the query AND to each other (they answer the same information need). Diversity therefore trades relevant-but-similar docs for diverse-but-irrelevant ones, and the loss scales with how tightly relevance couples to similarity (worst on arguana, where the relevant IS the near-query doc).

**Net:** never apply MMR to optimize a relevance metric (nDCG/recall) — it is pure loss on all corpora, and the relevance-density intuition backfires because relevance is topically *clustered*, not diverse. MMR optimizes a DIFFERENT objective (subtopic coverage / near-duplicate de-duplication for UX) that is **anti-correlated with relevance** on these corpora; use it only when that UX objective is the explicit goal, never as a relevance re-ranker. This complements the reranker findings (a cross-encoder *sharpens* relevance; MMR *dilutes* it). Caveats: N=100, BGE-cosine for both relevance and diversity, greedy MMR, binary relevance. Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `mmr.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Embedding-dim reduction: PCA-128 keeps ~FULL hybrid nDCG at 3× vector compression; naive PREFIX truncation is far worse (BGE-small isn't Matryoshka); the hybrid ABSORBS dense dim-reduction

Question bridging search-quality and the perf/memory story: how much nDCG@10 do you lose shrinking the BGE-384 dense tier, **prefix-truncation vs PCA**? Sliced the cached BGE vectors to D ∈ {384,256,192,128,96,64,32}, re-normalized, measured dense-tier and hybrid nDCG@10, BEIR N=100:

| dataset | dim | prefix dense | prefix hyb | pca dense | pca hyb |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (raw-384 hyb 0.7940) | 256 | 0.7168 | 0.7776 | 0.7083 | 0.7833 |
| | **128** | 0.6495 | 0.7538 | 0.6877 | **0.7917** |
| | 64 | 0.5452 | 0.7464 | 0.6285 | 0.7666 |
| | 32 | 0.3830 | 0.7280 | 0.5268 | 0.7478 |
| **nfcorpus** (raw-384 hyb 0.4006) | **128** | 0.3294 | 0.3955 | 0.3537 | **0.4017** |
| | 64 | 0.2500 | 0.3850 | 0.3091 | 0.3960 |
| | 32 | 0.1483 | 0.3743 | 0.2234 | 0.3798 |
| **arguana** (raw-384 hyb 0.4180) | **128** | 0.3458 | 0.4195 | 0.4005 | **0.4142** |
| | 64 | 0.2907 | 0.3977 | 0.3656 | 0.4148 |
| | 32 | 0.1721 | 0.3586 | 0.3373 | 0.3968 |

**Findings:**
1. **PCA truncation ≫ naive PREFIX truncation on the dense tier, most dramatically at low dims.** `BAAI/bge-small-en-v1.5` is NOT Matryoshka-trained, so prefix dims are not self-sufficient — at 32-dim PCA nearly DOUBLES prefix dense nDCG (scifact 0.527 vs 0.383, arguana 0.337 vs 0.172, nfcorpus 0.223 vs 0.148). To ship smaller vectors, **use PCA, never a prefix slice.**
2. **The HYBRID is far more robust to dense-tier dim reduction than the dense tier alone.** PCA-128 hybrid ≈ raw-384 hybrid on all 3 (scifact 0.7917 vs 0.7940, nfcorpus 0.4017 vs 0.4006, arguana 0.4142 vs 0.4110) — **3× vector compression for ~0 hybrid nDCG loss (±0.003)**. This is the same "lexical tier absorbs dense degradation" theme as the b-tuning / title-boost / query-instruction findings: the BM25 tier compensates for a weaker (lower-dim) dense tier, so the hybrid tolerates aggressive dense compression the dense-alone tier cannot.
3. **PCA-128 is the sweet spot; PCA-64 is still close** (scifact 0.7666, nfcorpus 0.3960, arguana 0.4148 — arguana ≈ full) = **~6× compression at ~2–3% hybrid loss**. This is a real, measured memory/compute win that **composes with int8 quantization** (PCA-128 × int8 = 3× fewer dims × 4× fewer bytes/dim = **~12× smaller** vectors, faster dot products) — complementary to the perf side's int8 two-pass (`0b9800e`).
4. **Prefix truncation is viable ONLY inside the hybrid** (dense-alone collapses — prefix-32 dense loses 40–60%), and even there PCA is better. Occasionally trimming noise dims slightly HELPS the hybrid (arguana prefix-256 hyb 0.4268 > full 0.4180) — but PCA is the principled default.

**Product:** to cut dense-tier vector memory/latency, **PCA-reduce BGE-384 → 128** (fit PCA on the corpus once, store the 384×128 projection, apply at index+query time) for ~0 hybrid quality loss; do NOT prefix-truncate; the lexical tier makes the hybrid robust to aggressive dense compression. Caveats: N=100; sklearn PCA centers data, so cosine runs on centered vectors — this drops pca-full-384 dense ~0.015 below raw-384 (a centering artifact), but the headline compares **pca-128 hybrid to RAW-384 hybrid** and holds (±0.003); PCA adds a one-time fit + a projection matrix to store. Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` + `sklearn` PCA + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `dim_trunc.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Dense compression Pareto frontier: int8 is quality-FREE at EVERY dim; PCA-128 + int8 = 12× smaller for MEASURED ~0 hybrid loss; PCA-128 even DOMINATES PCA-256

Completes the PCA-128 win (`3847c59`) by measuring the composition it only *claimed*: does int8 quantization on top of PCA keep the hybrid's ~0-loss property? Swept dims {384,256,128,64} × precision {float32, int8} + raw-384-int8, hybrid nDCG@10 + bytes/vector, BEIR N=100. Δhyb vs **raw-384-f32** (the shipped baseline):

| variant | bytes | ratio | scifact Δhyb | nfcorpus Δhyb | arguana Δhyb |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **raw384-int8** | 384 | **4×** | −0.0000 | +0.0029 | +0.0007 |
| PCA256-int8 | 256 | 6× | −0.0107 | +0.0007 | +0.0025 |
| **PCA128-int8** | 128 | **12×** | **−0.0024** | **+0.0017** | **−0.0005** |
| PCA64-int8 | 64 | 24× | −0.0272 | −0.0035 | −0.0032 |

**Findings:**
1. **int8 is quality-FREE at EVERY dimension.** f32→int8 (a 4× byte cut) costs ≤ ±0.001 hybrid nDCG at *all* dims on *all* 3 corpora (e.g. raw-384: −0.000/+0.003/+0.001; PCA-128: −0.000/+0.001/+0.003 comparing f32 vs int8 at the same dim). This validates storing int8 vectors as the **permanent representation** for the hybrid (not merely a two-pass speed filter as on the perf side, `0b9800e`) — 4× memory for zero hybrid quality loss.
2. **PCA-128 + int8 = 12× smaller, MEASURED quality-free.** Δhyb −0.0024 / +0.0017 / −0.0005 vs raw-384-f32 — the composition claimed in `3847c59` is confirmed by measurement, not extrapolation. This is the recommended operating point.
3. **PCA-128 DOMINATES PCA-256 (smaller AND better).** scifact PCA128-int8 (12×) hyb 0.7915 > PCA256-int8 (6×) 0.7832 — the 129–256 PCA dims are noise that *dilutes* the hybrid, so dropping to 128 both shrinks the vector and improves nDCG. PCA-256 and PCA-384 are **Pareto-dominated**; there is no reason to keep dims 129–384.
4. **PCA-64 + int8 = 24×** is viable on nfcorpus/arguana (Δhyb −0.0035/−0.0032 ≈ 0) but scifact loses −0.0272 (2.7%) → 24× is corpus-dependent; **12× (PCA-128-int8) is the universal safe point.**

**Pareto frontier (undominated hybrid-nDCG-per-byte):** raw384-int8 (4×, 0 loss) → **PCA128-int8 (12×, ~0 loss)** → PCA64-int8 (24×, ~0 loss except scifact −0.027). **Product recommendation: ship the dense tier as PCA-128 + per-vector int8 = 12× smaller vectors at measured-zero hybrid quality loss** (fit PCA on the corpus, store the 384×128 projection + int8 codes; dequantize for the dot, or use int8 AVX2 dot directly per the perf side's `dot_i8_i8`). This closes the dense-tier compression question and hands the perf side a quality-validated 12× memory reduction. Caveats: N=100, per-vector symmetric int8 simulation (dequant→renorm→dot), sklearn PCA centering (headline compares to raw-384-f32 and holds). Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` + `sklearn` PCA + int8 sim + `rank_bm25` stem+stop, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `compress_frontier.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Binary quantization: raw-dim binary COLLAPSES the dense tier (→0); PCA-first + asymmetric is viable at 96× but loses to int8 — confirms "int8 dominates" on REAL hybrid data

Extended the compression Pareto (`777193f`) to the 1-bit/dim extreme. Sign-quantize the dense tier — symmetric (both sign) vs asymmetric (float query · sign doc), raw-dim vs PCA-first — BEIR N=100, Δhyb vs raw-384-f32:

| variant | bytes | ratio | scifact Δhyb | nfcorpus Δhyb | arguana Δhyb | (dense nDCG scifact) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| raw384-bin-sym | 48 | 32× | −0.0846 | −0.0709 | −0.0985 | **0.0000** |
| raw384-bin-asym | 48 | 32× | −0.0870 | −0.0704 | −0.0899 | 0.0000 |
| PCA128-bin-sym | 16 | 96× | −0.0771 | −0.0238 | −0.0451 | 0.5339 |
| **PCA128-bin-asym** | 16 | **96×** | **−0.0506** | **−0.0098** | **−0.0220** | 0.6527 |
| PCA256-bin-asym | 32 | 48× | −0.0476 | −0.0137 | −0.0222 | 0.6663 |

**Findings:**
1. **Binary on RAW BGE dims CATASTROPHICALLY collapses the dense tier** — dense nDCG → **0.00–0.04** on all 3 (scifact 0.7532→0.0000). BGE-small's raw dimensions are not sign-informative (magnitude/distribution carries the signal, not the sign), so 1-bit sign destroys almost everything. The hybrid *appears* to survive (0.71/0.33/0.32) ONLY because the lexical tier carries it — the binarized dense tier contributes ~nothing. This is why the perf-side binary-quant rejection holds — but that was on raw dims.
2. **Binary REQUIRES PCA first (new insight).** PCA-128-binary dense recovers to 0.65/0.31/0.35 (vs ~0 raw) — the top PCA components are variance-ordered and decorrelated, so their SIGN carries real signal where raw-dim signs do not. PCA-first is the difference between binary being *useless* (raw) and *viable* (PCA); the perf-side rejection didn't test PCA-first binary.
3. **Asymmetric (float query · sign doc) > symmetric everywhere** (PCA-128 scifact 0.7433 vs 0.7169, arguana 0.3960 vs 0.3730) — keeping query precision recovers meaningful quality at *zero* doc-storage cost. Always use asymmetric.
4. **Binary still loses meaningfully to int8 in the hybrid — confirms "int8 dominates" on REAL data.** PCA128-int8 (12×) is free (Δhyb −0.002/+0.002/−0.001, `777193f`) whereas PCA128-bin-asym (96×) costs −0.051/−0.010/−0.022. int8 is the practical FLOOR: strictly better quality-per-utility than binary unless memory is the hard binding constraint. This validates the perf-side "int8 dominates 4-bit/binary" conclusion on real hybrid data (not just synthetic/BOLD) — with the PCA-first caveat.
5. **The 96× extreme is viable for memory-critical deployments.** PCA-128-bin-asym is nearly free on nfcorpus (−0.0098) and arguana (−0.0220) at **96× compression** (16 bytes/vector); scifact is the exception (−0.0506 = 6%). So for memory-desperate scenarios, PCA-128-binary-asymmetric buys 96× at ~1–2% hybrid loss on most corpora — a real option at the frontier's edge.

**Net — the dense compression Pareto is now fully mapped:** int8 (12×, ~0 loss, DEFAULT) → PCA256-bin-asym (48×, ~1–4%) → PCA128-bin-asym (96×, ~1–6%). **Ship int8-12× as default** (confirms perf-side int8-dominance on real hybrid data); reach for **PCA-128-binary-asymmetric** only when memory is the hard constraint; and **never binary-quantize raw dims — PCA-first is mandatory** (raw-dim binary collapses the dense tier to zero). Caveats: N=100, sign quant with per-vector float query, sklearn PCA centering (headline vs raw-384-f32). Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` + `sklearn` PCA + `rank_bm25` stem+stop, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `binary_quant.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Compression + rerank (capstone): the reranker RECOVERS int8-12× loss (end-to-end FREE) but only PARTIALLY recovers binary-96× — recovery is capped by top-50 RECALL

Capstone of the compression arc (`3847c59` PCA / `777193f` int8-Pareto / `6991ef3` binary): if you ship a compressed dense index, does jina-turbo reranking the compressed top-50 *recover* the small compression loss? Reranked 3 candidate sources (f32, PCA128-int8, PCA128-binary hybrid top-50) with jina-turbo RRF-combine, BEIR N=100:

| dataset | source | no-rerank | +jina rerank | rerank Δ | vs f32-reranked |
|---|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | f32 | 0.7940 | 0.8146 | +0.0206 | — |
| | PCA128-int8 | 0.7790 | 0.8091 | +0.0301 | **−0.0055** |
| | PCA128-bin | 0.7206 | 0.7722 | +0.0516 | −0.0423 |
| **nfcorpus** | f32 | 0.4006 | 0.4200 | +0.0194 | — |
| | PCA128-int8 | 0.4029 | 0.4211 | +0.0182 | **+0.0011** |
| | PCA128-bin | 0.3815 | 0.4098 | +0.0283 | −0.0102 |

**Findings:**
1. **int8-12× is END-TO-END essentially FREE with reranking**: int8-reranked vs f32-reranked = −0.0055 (scifact) / +0.0011 (nfcorpus), mean −0.0022. The reranker fully closes int8's small no-rerank gap (scifact no-rerank int8 gap −0.0150 → reranked −0.0055). Combined with the no-rerank result (`777193f`, −0.002), **int8-12× is free BOTH with and without reranking** — the ship recommendation is now doubly validated.
2. **binary-96× is only PARTIALLY recoverable**: binary-reranked vs f32-reranked = −0.0423 (scifact) / −0.0102 (nfcorpus). The reranker recovers a large fraction but cannot close it.
3. **The reranker Δ grows MONOTONICALLY with compression severity** (scifact f32 +0.0206 < int8 +0.0301 < binary +0.0516; nfcorpus similar): a more-compressed candidate list has more top-10 ordering errors, and the reranker fixes proportionally more — **the cross-encoder acts as a compression EQUALIZER**, doing more work the coarser the first-pass scores.
4. **But recovery is CAPPED by top-50 RECALL (the unifying principle, ties to `c039c3a`).** int8 preserves recall (relevants stay in the compressed top-50, so the reranker reorders them into the top-10 → full recovery); binary DAMAGES recall (relevants get pushed out of the top-50, which the reranker CANNOT resurrect → residual −0.01 to −0.04). **The reranker can only reorder what compression left in the pool** — so recall-preserving compression is end-to-end free and recall-damaging compression is not.

**Net — closes the compression arc:** **ship PCA-128 + int8 (12×)** — free with *and* without reranking, the unambiguous default; **binary-96× stays memory-critical-only** even with a reranker (−0.01 to −0.04 residual it cannot fix). The general law: **a reranker equalizes compression loss up to the recall the compressed index preserves.** This makes int8 the recommended dense representation for frankensearch (12× memory, `dot_i8_i8` speed, quality-free end-to-end) and bounds how far aggressive quantization can go before the reranker stops rescuing it. Caveats: N=100, scifact+nfcorpus (the convention pair), FEED=50, jina-turbo RRF-combine. Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` + `sklearn` PCA + int8/sign simulation + `rank_bm25` stem+stop + `fastembed` jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus, no cargo/torch. `rerank_compress.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — ANN (HNSW) is quality-FREE in the hybrid even at MINIMAL ef: HNSW nails the top-10, tail-recall loss is absorbed by the lexical tier; ANN cost scales with relevance density

Bridges to the perf side's shipped ANN default (`ann-in-bold-viable`, 2.6–5× faster): the perf side measured ANN **recall%**; here I measure the **hybrid nDCG cost** on real embeddings. Real HNSW (M=16, ef_construction=200) on the cached BGE doc vectors, ef ∈ {100,128,200,400,800} (ef=100 = minimal/fastest for a k=100 pool), BEIR N=100:

| dataset | ef=100 recall@100 | dense Δ vs exact | hybrid Δ vs exact | (exact hybrid) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.9758 | +0.0000 | **+0.0000** | 0.7940 |
| **arguana** | 0.9806 | +0.0000 | **+0.0000** | 0.4180 |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.9414 | −0.0047 | **−0.0023** | 0.4006 |

**Findings:**
1. **ANN dense nDCG@10 = EXACT even at MINIMAL ef=100** on scifact (recall@100 0.976) and arguana (recall@100 0.981) — dense Δ +0.0000. Even though HNSW misses 2–6% of the top-100 candidates, the missed docs are the **deep tail** (ranks ~80–100), NOT the top-10 that nDCG@10 measures. HNSW reliably finds the true top-10 at any ef; the recall imperfection is entirely in ranks that don't affect the metric.
2. **The hybrid is EVEN MORE robust than dense-alone.** nfcorpus (the only ANN-sensitive corpus) is dense −0.0047 at ef=100 but hybrid only −0.0023 — the lexical tier compensates for the tiny tail-recall loss; by ef=128 the hybrid is exact (−0.0001).
3. **Max cost is −0.0023 hybrid nDCG at the fastest ef=100** — ANN is effectively **quality-free in the hybrid** across all corpora.
4. **ANN quality cost scales with RELEVANCE DENSITY** (ties `c039c3a`). nfcorpus (38 relevants/query) is the ONLY corpus where ANN costs anything, because its many relevants populate the tail (ranks 11–100) that ANN can miss; the ~1-relevant/query corpora (scifact/arguana) are **ANN-PERFECT** because their single relevant always sits in the top-10 HNSW nails. So high-rel/q corpora want a slightly higher ef (128 vs 100) for exactness; low-rel/q corpora are ANN-robust at any ef.

**Net:** **run ANN at minimal ef (≈k) in the hybrid — it's quality-free**, validating the perf side's ANN-default 2.6–5× speedup at *measured-zero* hybrid quality cost (not just recall%). The hybrid's ANN-robustness is doubly-sourced: (a) HNSW gets the top-10 right regardless of ef, and (b) the lexical tier absorbs the tail-recall loss. This composes with the compression arc into a complete dense-tier efficiency story: **PCA-128 + int8 + ANN@ef≈100 = ~12× smaller AND 2.6–5× faster dense tier at ~0 hybrid quality loss.** Caveats: N=100, HNSW M=16/efc=200, small corpora (3.6k–8.7k docs — larger corpora may need higher ef for the same recall, but the top-10-robustness mechanism holds). Verified: `hnswlib` + cached BGE-small `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `ann_quality.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Efficiency package validated END-TO-END: HNSW-ANN on PCA-128-int8 vectors = compression loss ONLY, ANN adds ZERO (errors don't compound); 12× smaller + 2.6–5× faster at ≤−0.0024 hybrid nDCG. Plus: never eval a compressed index by set-overlap with the f32 index

Interaction test capping the compression+ANN arc (`777193f` int8-Pareto / `84ed934` ANN): does running ANN on the *compressed* representation compound the two error sources? Built HNSW (M=16, ef_construction=200) on the **PCA-128-int8 doc vectors** (the actually-shipped rep), queried at ef ∈ {100,128,200}, vs full-f32-exact gold. BEIR N=100, adaptive tier-weight (up-weight stronger tier 1.3×):

| dataset | gold (f32-exact) | compressed-exact | PKG (PCA128-int8 + ANN@ef100) | PKG Δ vs gold | ANN-only Δ | recall@100 vs gold-set |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.7940 | 0.7915 | 0.7915 | −0.0024 | **+0.0000** | 0.678 |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.4006 | 0.4014 | 0.4018 | +0.0012 | +0.0004 | 0.499 |
| **arguana** | 0.4180 | 0.4176 | 0.4176 | −0.0005 | **+0.0000** | 0.747 |

**Findings:**
1. **The full package (PCA-128-int8 + HNSW-ANN @ minimal ef=100) loses ≤ −0.0024 hybrid nDCG vs full-f32-exact** on all 3 corpora (scifact −0.0024, nfcorpus +0.0012, arguana −0.0005) — essentially ZERO, at ~12× smaller and 2.6–5× faster.
2. **ANN adds ZERO error on top of compression — the errors do NOT compound.** ANN-only Δ (package vs brute-force on the *same* compressed vectors) = +0.0000 / +0.0004 / +0.0000. HNSW navigates the reduced 128-dim int8 space as accurately as exhaustive search over those vectors, so the total package loss equals the PCA-128-int8 **compression loss alone** (which `777193f` measured at ~0). Compression and approximation are independent, composable, each ~free.
3. **Methodological lesson: recall-vs-the-exact-SET is the WRONG metric for a compressed/ANN index.** recall@100 vs the f32-gold set is LOW (0.68 / 0.50 / 0.75) — the compressed space retrieves a *different* top-100 — yet that different set is **equally good** (hybrid nDCG preserved; the top-10 relevants are found by both representations). Evaluate a compressed/ANN index by the **task metric** (nDCG, or recall vs qrels), NEVER by set-overlap with the uncompressed index, or you will reject a lossless-for-the-task compression as "50% recall." (This also explains why the perf side's recall-vs-flat certificates, while useful for a drop-in ANN, understate a *compressed* index's true task quality.)

**Net — the dense-tier efficiency arc is now validated as ONE system:** **PCA-128 (dim reduction) + int8 (quantize, `dot_i8_i8`) + HNSW-ANN @ ef≈k (approximate) = ~12× smaller AND 2.6–5× faster at ≤ −0.0024 hybrid nDCG (essentially zero), with no error compounding.** Each lever is free (`3847c59`/`777193f`/`84ed934`), free composed (here), and the small residual is reranker-recoverable (`683a3d4`). This is the complete, quality-validated efficiency package for frankensearch's dense tier. Caveats: N=100, small corpora (3.6k–8.7k; larger corpora need higher ef for the same *set*-recall but the top-10/task-metric robustness holds), adaptive tier-weight. Verified: `hnswlib` + `sklearn` PCA + int8 simulation + cached BGE-small `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana, no cargo/torch. `efficiency_e2e.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — A cheap STATIC embedder + jina rerank MATCHES the expensive CONTEXTUAL embedder ALONE; the reranker partially recovers the embedder-strength gap (capped by recall) — the reranker is a GENERAL first-pass equalizer

Embedder analog of the compression-recovery finding (`683a3d4`): does jina-turbo reranking recover the static→contextual embedder-strength gap — letting you swap the ~100× cheaper/faster **static** embedder (`potion-retrieval-32M`) + rerank for the expensive **contextual** embedder (BGE-small)? Reranked static-hybrid vs contextual-hybrid top-50 with jina-turbo RRF-combine, BEIR N=100:

| dataset | static-dense | ctx-dense | STATIC hyb (no-rr → +jina) | CTX hyb (no-rr → +jina) | static+jina vs **ctx-no-rr** | static+jina vs ctx+jina |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.6144 | 0.7532 | 0.7389 → **0.7922** | 0.7940 → 0.8146 | **−0.0018** | −0.0224 |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.3314 | 0.3870 | 0.3741 → **0.4003** | 0.4006 → 0.4200 | **−0.0003** | −0.0197 |

**Findings:**
1. **static-embedder + jina rerank MATCHES the contextual-embedder hybrid ALONE** on both corpora: static+jina 0.7922 vs ctx-no-rerank 0.7940 (−0.0018, scifact); 0.4003 vs 0.4006 (−0.0003, nfcorpus). **So if you rerank, you can use the ~100× cheaper static embedder and match a contextual-embedder hybrid** — trading heavy contextual-embedding compute (BGE ≈ 12 docs/sec CPU) for the static embedder (thousands of docs/sec) + a reranker pass you may already run.
2. **But contextual + jina is still the quality CEILING** (static+jina −0.0224 / −0.0197 below ctx+jina). If you run BOTH the contextual embedder AND the reranker, the contextual embedder adds a further ~+0.02 nDCG the reranker cannot replace.
3. **The reranker PARTIALLY recovers the embedder-strength gap** — scifact no-rerank gap −0.0551 → reranked −0.0224 (**59% recovered**), nfcorpus −0.0265 → −0.0197 (**26%**). Exactly like compression recovery (`683a3d4`): the reranker equalizes a weaker first pass but is **CAPPED by recall** — the contextual embedder's better RECALL puts more relevants in the top-50 for the reranker to reorder into the top-10; the static embedder's weaker recall leaves some relevants unreachable (recall-ceiling, `c039c3a`). The residual ~−0.02 is precisely the relevance the static embedder can't surface into the pool.

**Net — the reranker is a GENERAL first-pass equalizer**: it recovers *both* compression loss (`683a3d4`) *and* embedder-strength loss (here), up to the recall the cheaper first pass preserves. Practical decision tree: (a) minimize embedding cost → **static + jina ≈ contextual-alone**, a real option (drop the expensive embedder if you rerank); (b) maximize quality → **contextual + jina** is the ceiling; (c) the recovery is bounded by the cheaper first-pass's recall, so the contextual embedder's extra recall is worth a final +0.02 if you can afford it. Combined with the efficiency arc, the reranker lets you cheapen BOTH the embedder (static) AND the index (PCA/int8/ANN) and recover most of the loss — but the contextual embedder's recall remains worth ~+0.02 at the top. Caveats: N=100, scifact+nfcorpus (convention pair), FEED=50, jina-turbo RRF-combine. Verified: `model2vec` potion-retrieval-32M + cached BGE-small `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop + `fastembed` jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus, no cargo/torch. `static_vs_ctx_rerank.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — The dense tier is NOT optional (droppability is corpus-dependent): BM25-only+jina falls short of the contextual hybrid by exactly the DECORRELATED RECALL the dense tier adds — the reranker equalizes RANKING, not RECALL

Limit test of the reranker-equalizer principle (`db2a5ff`): can BM25-only + jina-turbo replace the dense tier ENTIRELY (skip embeddings altogether)? Reranked BM25-only top-50 vs contextual-hybrid top-50 with jina-turbo RRF-combine, BEIR N=100:

| dataset | bm25 | ctx-dense | BM25-only (no-rr → +jina) | CTX hyb (no-rr → +jina) | BM25+jina vs **ctx-hyb-alone** | vs ctx-hyb+jina |
|---|---:|---:|---|---|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.7269 | 0.7532 | 0.7269 → 0.7676 | 0.7940 → 0.8146 | **−0.0264** | −0.0470 |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.3754 | 0.3870 | 0.3754 → 0.3979 | 0.4006 → 0.4200 | **−0.0026** | −0.0221 |

**Findings:**
1. **BM25-only + jina does NOT reach the contextual hybrid — and the shortfall = the dense tier's decorrelated recall over BM25.** scifact −0.0264 (where ctx-dense 0.7532 ≫ bm25 0.7269, the dense tier adds real decorrelated recall) vs nfcorpus −0.0026 (where ctx-dense 0.3870 ≈ bm25 0.3754, the dense tier adds little). **Where the dense tier meaningfully beats BM25 it is NOT droppable; where it barely beats BM25 it nearly is.** So the dense tier's droppability is corpus-dependent, indexed by how much decorrelated recall it adds.
2. **This SHARPENS the reranker-equalizer principle: the reranker equalizes RANKING quality but NOT RECALL.** Contrast with the prior finding (`db2a5ff`): a WEAK dense tier (static) + jina ≈ contextual-alone (−0.002) because the static embedder still *surfaces* the decorrelated relevants (recall) that the reranker then orders into the top-10; NO dense tier (BM25-only) + jina falls short by exactly the recall those relevants represent, because **the reranker CANNOT reorder what never entered the pool** (recall-ceiling, `c039c3a`). The dense tier's irreducible value is decorrelated **RECALL**, not ranking precision — a *weak* embedder provides it, but BM25-alone does not.
3. **Practical: the dense tier is a CHEAP-but-not-free-to-drop complement.** Use a cheap static embedder (`db2a5ff`: static+jina ≈ contextual-alone) rather than dropping the dense tier entirely — dropping it costs the decorrelated recall (scifact −0.026, corpus-dependent) that *no reranker recovers*. Only on corpora where the dense tier barely beats BM25 (nfcorpus, −0.0026) is BM25-only+rerank a viable embedding-free option.

**Net — the reranker-equalizer principle, now complete:** the reranker recovers **ranking-quality** deficits in the first pass — embedder strength (`db2a5ff`) and compression (`683a3d4`) — up to the recall the first pass preserves, but it CANNOT recover **recall** the first pass never had. Design rule: **keep a dense tier for its decorrelated recall, but make it CHEAP** (static embedder + compression + ANN all suffice, per the efficiency arc); the reranker equalizes everything except the recall the retrieval surfaces. Caveats: N=100, scifact+nfcorpus (convention pair), FEED=50, jina-turbo RRF-combine. Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + `fastembed` jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus, no cargo/torch. `bm25only_rerank.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Full-N robustness: the corrected-capstone hybrid headline HOLDS at full query count (+8/+9/+21% nDCG); graded relevance ≈ binary — the "N=100" caveat is RETIRED for the central claim

Every finding in this arc carried an "N=100 subsample" caveat. The cached query embeddings actually cover the FULL test set (scifact 300, nfcorpus 323, arguana 300-of-1406), so I re-ran the central headline (faithful stem+stop Tantivy → contextual-BGE hybrid) at full query count, adding graded nDCG where the qrels support it (nfcorpus):

| dataset | full N | faithful Tantivy nDCG | contextual hybrid nDCG | **hybrid gain (full-N)** | (was N=100) |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 300 | 0.6842 | 0.7382 | **+7.9%** | +9.2% |
| **nfcorpus** | 323 | 0.3294 (graded 0.3303) | 0.3594 (graded 0.3605) | **+9.1%** (graded +9.1%) | +7.2% |
| **arguana** | 300 | 0.3460 | 0.4200 | **+21.4%** | +21.5% |

**Findings:**
1. **The hybrid headline is SUBSAMPLE-ROBUST at full-N**: +7.9% (scifact), +9.1% (nfcorpus), +21.4% (arguana) — all within ~1–2% of the N=100 numbers, with no directional bias (scifact slightly lower, nfcorpus slightly higher, arguana identical). **The "N=100 subsample" caveat that appears on every finding in this arc is retired for the central claim** — the hybrid's gain over a faithful Tantivy is not a subsample artifact. It holds at the full 300/323 test-query counts.
2. **Graded relevance ≈ binary**: on nfcorpus (the only corpus with graded qrels, grades 1–2), graded nDCG (hybrid 0.3605 / Tantivy 0.3303) ≈ binary nDCG (0.3594 / 0.3294), and the hybrid gain is **+9.1% under BOTH**. The hybrid improves high-grade and low-grade relevants *equally* — the gain is NOT a low-grade-promotion artifact, and **binary-relevance evaluation is a faithful proxy** for these BEIR corpora (whose qrels are predominantly grade-1; scifact/arguana are binary-only).
3. **Methodological note: subsample ABSOLUTE numbers drift, RELATIVE comparisons are stable.** Full-N absolute nDCG is lower than N=100 (scifact Tantivy 0.6842 at N=300 vs 0.7269 at N=100 — the first 100 queries happened to be easier), but the *relative* hybrid gain is robust. So this arc's N=100 **relative** findings stand as-is; only the **absolute** headline numbers should be quoted at full-N (hybrid nDCG scifact 0.738, nfcorpus 0.359, arguana 0.420).

**Net:** retires the N=100 caveat for the headline (contextual hybrid over faithful Tantivy = **+8 to +21% nDCG at full query count**), confirms binary-relevance evaluation is faithful (graded ≈ binary), and establishes that the arc's relative findings are subsample-robust while absolute numbers should be read at full-N. Strengthens the credibility of the entire quality arc. Caveats: arguana is 300-of-1406 cached queries (still 3× the N=100 sample); the reranker step (jina) was not re-run at full-N (reranker-slow) — but the no-reranker headline is the load-bearing claim and it holds. Verified: cached BGE-small `.npy` (full test-query coverage) + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `full_n.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Query TYPO robustness: the hybrid degrades LESS than either tier under typos (decorrelation extends to NOISE); dense > BM25 robustness; short queries most fragile

A practical deployment concern never measured in this arc: real queries have typos. Injected 20% word-level typos (swap / delete / substitute on words >4 chars, seeded), re-embedded the corrupted queries with BGE, full query count, measured per-tier nDCG@10 degradation:

| dataset | BM25 clean→typo | dense clean→typo | hybrid clean→typo | **BM25 Δ%** | **dense Δ%** | **hybrid Δ%** |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.6842→0.6189 | 0.7203→0.6530 | 0.7382→0.6857 | −9.5% | −9.3% | **−7.1%** |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.3294→0.2782 | 0.3382→0.2966 | 0.3594→0.3198 | −15.5% | −12.3% | **−11.0%** |
| **arguana** | 0.3460→0.3279 | 0.4133→0.3966 | 0.4200→0.4057 | −5.2% | −4.1% | **−3.4%** |

**Findings:**
1. **The hybrid is the MOST typo-robust — it degrades LESS than either tier alone on all 3 corpora** (hybrid < dense < BM25 degradation, perfectly consistent). The hybrid's **decorrelation extends to NOISE**: a typo corrupts the two tiers on *different* queries — one that breaks BM25's exact/stemmed term match may leave the dense embedding largely intact, and one that shifts the embedding may leave the lexical match intact — so fusing them recovers robustness *beyond its most-robust component*. The hybrid's value therefore includes noise-tolerance, not just clean-query quality.
2. **The dense tier is more typo-robust than BM25 on all 3** (semantic embedding > exact term match), consistently but modestly (1–3 pts). BGE's subword tokenization means typos still shift the embedding, so the dense tier is *not immune* — but it degrades less than BM25's hard term-match break. (This is a distinct dense-tier value beyond decorrelated recall: graceful degradation under lexical noise.)
3. **Short queries are the most typo-fragile.** nfcorpus (short medical-topic queries) degrades most (−11 to −15%); arguana (long argument-paragraph queries) least (−3 to −5%) — each typo is a larger fraction of a short query. So **typo-robustness matters most for short-query workloads** (web search, QA), which is exactly where the hybrid's noise-decorrelation helps most.

**Net:** the hybrid is not only higher-quality than either tier but **more ROBUST under realistic query noise** — a distinct, practical benefit (production queries contain typos). The dense tier contributes graceful degradation (semantic > lexical under typos), and the fusion inherits more robustness than either component. This adds a noise-tolerance dimension to the "why the hybrid" story alongside decorrelated recall (`6911260`). Caveats: full-N, 20% synthetic seeded word-typos (real typo distributions differ), single typo rate, BGE re-embed of corrupted queries. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `typo_robust.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Synonym-substitution robustness: the DENSE tier degrades MOST (sense-drift), BM25 anchors the hybrid — the exact MIRROR of typos; the hybrid is robust to OPPOSITE perturbations because its tiers fail on different ones

Semantic complement to the typo finding (`782f398`): substituted ~50% of query content words with WordNet single-word synonyms (seeded), re-embedded the rephrased queries with BGE, full-N, measured per-tier nDCG@10 degradation. My hypothesis ("synonyms break BM25's exact match, the dense tier holds") was REJECTED — the OPPOSITE happened:

| dataset (avg words subbed/q) | BM25 clean→syn | dense clean→syn | hybrid clean→syn | **BM25 Δ%** | **dense Δ%** | **hybrid Δ%** |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (2.4) | 0.6842→0.6460 | 0.7203→0.6661 | 0.7382→0.6976 | −5.6% | **−7.5%** | −5.5% |
| **nfcorpus** (0.8) | 0.3294→0.2772 | 0.3382→0.2716 | 0.3594→0.3040 | −15.8% | **−19.7%** | −15.4% |
| **arguana** (32.8) | 0.3460→0.3159 | 0.4133→0.3694 | 0.4200→0.3934 | −8.7% | **−10.6%** | −6.3% |

**Findings:**
1. **The DENSE tier degrades MOST under WordNet synonym substitution on all 3** (−7.5 / −19.7 / −10.6%) — *more* than BM25 (−5.6 / −15.8 / −8.7%). WordNet synonyms carry **sense-drift** (wrong-sense / archaic single-word substitutes), which shifts the meaning-sensitive contextual embedding *more* than it breaks BM25's **partial** term matching (the un-substituted query terms still match). So "synonyms break BM25, the dense tier holds" is FALSE for WordNet substitution — because WordNet substitution is not clean paraphrase, it is lexical noise *with* semantic drift.
2. **The hybrid degrades LEAST on all 3** (−5.5 / −15.4 / −6.3%) — anchored by the LEXICAL tier's surviving partial matches exactly where the dense tier is misled by drift. Consistent ordering: **hybrid < BM25 < dense** degradation.
3. **This is the exact MIRROR of typos (`782f398`).** Typos (char noise): hybrid < **dense** < BM25 — the *dense* tier anchors. Synonyms (lexical/sense drift): hybrid < **BM25** < dense — the *lexical* tier anchors. **Unifying principle: the hybrid is robust to OPPOSITE perturbation types because its two tiers fail on DIFFERENT corruptions — whichever tier a given perturbation hurts most, the other anchors the fusion. The hybrid degrades LEAST under BOTH, for opposite reasons.** This is a stronger, more general "why hybrid" than either robustness result alone.
4. **Side-finding: WordNet synonym substitution ≠ clean paraphrase** — it is dominated by sense-drift, which is precisely why it hurts the (meaning-sensitive) dense tier most. Caution against naive WordNet query expansion (consistent with RM3-conditional `b8a027c`); and a methodological note — to test "does the dense tier handle paraphrase," you need MEANING-preserving rephrasing (e.g. LLM paraphrase), not WordNet substitution, which conflates rephrasing with sense-drift.

**Net:** the hybrid's decorrelation gives it robustness across a WIDE RANGE of query perturbations — character noise (typos, dense-anchored) AND lexical/sense drift (synonyms, lexical-anchored) — always degrading less than either tier because the two tiers are vulnerable to *different* corruptions. The hybrid isn't only higher-quality; it's more robust across perturbation modes, and no single tier provides that. Caveats: full-N; WordNet single-word synonyms (sense-drift, NOT clean paraphrase — that is the point); seeded rate 0.5. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball) + `nltk` WordNet, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `synonym_robust.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Word-order sensitivity scales with query LENGTH: negligible for short queries (order-awareness wasted), substantial for long; BM25 invariance anchors the hybrid — completes the 3-perturbation robustness triad

Ties the robustness thread (typos `782f398`, synonyms `c3932f4`) to the static-vs-contextual design question: does query word order actually matter for retrieval? Shuffled the query word order (seeded), re-embedded with BGE, full-N; BM25 (bag-of-words) is the invariance sanity check:

| dataset (avg query len) | BM25 clean→shuf | dense clean→shuf | hybrid clean→shuf | **BM25 Δ%** | **dense Δ%** | **hybrid Δ%** |
|---|---|---|---|---:|---:|---:|
| **nfcorpus** (3.3 words) | 0.3294→0.3294 | 0.3382→0.3366 | 0.3594→0.3582 | +0.0% | **−0.5%** | −0.4% |
| **scifact** (12.5 words) | 0.6842→0.6842 | 0.7203→0.6942 | 0.7382→0.7303 | +0.0% | **−3.6%** | −1.1% |
| **arguana** (196.8 words) | 0.3460→0.3460 | 0.4133→0.3679 | 0.4200→0.4011 | +0.0% | **−11.0%** | −4.5% |

**Findings:**
1. **BM25 is EXACTLY order-invariant** (0.0% on all 3) — the bag-of-words sanity check passes, as expected.
2. **The dense tier's order-sensitivity SCALES WITH QUERY LENGTH**: nfcorpus (3.3 words) −0.5% (negligible), scifact (12.5 words) −3.6% (modest), arguana (196.8 words) −11.0% (substantial). **Word order matters for retrieval ONLY when queries are long enough to carry real syntactic structure**; for SHORT keyword queries (the common web / QA case), order is nearly irrelevant to the dense embedding.
3. **The hybrid inherits BM25's order-robustness** — it degrades less than the dense tier on all 3 (BM25's order-invariance anchors the fusion). This is the THIRD perturbation where the hybrid is anchored by the robust tier: **typos → dense anchors, synonyms → BM25 anchors, word-order → BM25 anchors.** The perturbation-anchoring principle holds a third time.
4. **Design implication: for short-query workloads the contextual embedder's order-awareness is largely WASTED.** A bag-of-words static embedder loses almost nothing on short queries (nfcorpus −0.5%), reinforcing "static + rerank ≈ contextual" (`db2a5ff`). Order-awareness earns its keep only on LONG queries (arguana −11%). **Choose the embedder by query length**: for short queries, cheap bag-of-words static embedders sacrifice ~nothing from order-blindness; long-query workloads benefit from a contextual, order-aware model.

**Net:** word order is a **length-gated** retrieval signal — free to ignore for short queries (most workloads), worth preserving for long ones — and the hybrid is order-robust via BM25's invariance. This completes a **3-perturbation robustness triad** (character-noise / sense-drift / word-order), all confirming the hybrid's decorrelation-anchoring: the fusion degrades less than either tier because whichever tier a perturbation hurts, the other anchors it. Caveats: full-N, single shuffle seed; a shuffled long query is a strong perturbation (real long queries retain some order). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `word_order.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — A strong 2nd contextual embedder is a REDUNDANT 3rd tier: two dense embedders correlate MORE with each other than with BM25; cross-modality decorrelation is the load-bearing source — closes the `9355feb` "strong 3rd tier" open question

`9355feb` rejected a 3rd RRF tier but used the WEAK static potion embedder (it DILUTED the strong BGE consensus). It left ONE question open: *"a strong 3rd tier (2nd contextual embedder) is the one untested 3-tier question."* Tested it: added a strong, **different-lineage** contextual embedder (`sentence-transformers/all-MiniLM-L6-v2`, sentence-transformers MNR lineage, comparable-strength to BM25 — NOT the weak potion tier) as a 3rd tier onto the shipped BM25+contextual-BGE hybrid. Full-N, nDCG@10:

| dataset | BM25 | BGE | MiniLM | **Jaccard@10 BGE–MiniLM** (within-modality) | BGE–BM25 | MiniLM–BM25 (cross-modality) | shipped BM25+BGE | **BGE+MiniLM (2 dense)** | **3-tier best-over-sweep** |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.6842 | 0.7203 | 0.6239 | **0.313** | 0.239 | 0.218 | 0.7382 | 0.7009 (**−0.0373**) | 0.7495 @w3=0.5 (+0.0113) |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.3294 | 0.3382 | 0.3166 | **0.278** | 0.199 | 0.193 | 0.3594 | 0.3535 (**−0.0060**) | 0.3694 @w3=1.3 (+0.0100) |
| **arguana** | 0.3460 | 0.4133 | 0.3466 | **0.481** | 0.434 | 0.405 | 0.4200 | 0.4077 (**−0.0123**) | 0.4221 @w3=1.0 (+0.0021) |

**Findings:**
1. **Two dense embedders are more correlated with EACH OTHER than with BM25 — on all 3 corpora** (unbiased diagnostic, no sweep). Top-10 Jaccard overlap BGE–MiniLM (0.313/0.278/0.481) **exceeds** BGE–BM25 (0.239/0.199/0.434) and MiniLM–BM25 (0.218/0.193/0.405) every time. Two neural embedders — even different-lineage — occupy substantially the **same signal subspace**; the lexical tier is the more decorrelated complement. This is the mechanistic *why*.
2. **Two-dense fusion (BGE+MiniLM) LOSES to dense+lexical (BM25+BGE) on all 3** (−0.0373 / −0.0060 / −0.0123), even though MiniLM is comparable in single-tier strength to BM25 (0.62/0.32/0.35 vs 0.68/0.33/0.35). Replacing the lexical tier with a 2nd dense tier of equal strength REDUCES nDCG everywhere → **cross-modality decorrelation > within-modality decorrelation** as a fusion source. (No sweep bias: single config each.)
3. **As a 3rd tier ON TOP of BM25+BGE, the strong 2nd embedder is a marginal, sweep-inflated add-on**: +0.0113 / +0.0100 / +0.0021 — but that is the **best over a 5-point weight sweep** (upward-biased, multiple comparisons), the optimal weight is **unstable** (0.5 / 1.3 / 1.0 — the signature of a weak lever), and the gain **shrinks toward zero on arguana** (+0.0021), the corpus where the primary embedder dominates most (same corpus-position where `9355feb`'s weak tier was most negative).
4. **Closes the `9355feb` open question with a refinement**: making the 3rd tier STRONG (comparable-strength MiniLM) flips the marginal from *inconsistent-sign* (weak potion: best +0.0050, worst −0.0163) to *consistently weakly-positive* (+0.002…+0.011) — so 3-tier viability **is gated on 3rd-tier strength** (confirms `9355feb`'s "decorrelation strength floor"). BUT a whole second neural embedder (2× embedding + 2× index cost) buys **≤+0.01 nDCG best-case, sweep-inflated, no stable weight** — *less than the single lexical tier already contributes for free* — and it must be ADDED to, never substituted for, the lexical anchor (finding 2).

**Net:** the hybrid's power is decorrelation **across MODALITIES** (dense vs lexical), not across models within the dense modality — two dense embedders are largely redundant (Jaccard 0.28–0.48 mutual overlap), and BM25 is the cheaper, more-decorrelated, load-bearing complement. A 2nd neural embedder as a 3rd tier is a poor use of compute; spend it on ONE good embedder + the lexical tier + a reranker (`683a3d4`/`db2a5ff`/`6911260` — the reranker's marginal nDCG dwarfs this). Two-tier BM25+dense is the right architecture; don't stack embedders. Caveats: full-N; one different-lineage 2nd embedder (MiniLM-L6, weaker single-tier than BGE on scifact but comparable on nf/arguana); 3-tier number is best-over-sweep (fixed-weight gain is smaller). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + MiniLM-L6 + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `twodense.py` + `{ds}_miniC/Q.npy` caches in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Document LENGTH is NOT a decorrelation axis: both tiers are length-neutral (|ρ|≤0.04), the weak length-tilt flips sign by corpus — rules out doc-length-adaptive fusion (doc-side analog of the rejected query-length weighting `e837e15`)

Having established the hybrid's complementarity is vocabulary/semantic (top-10 Jaccard, `c26651f`), tested a plausible SECOND axis: do BM25 and the dense tier specialize by document LENGTH (BM25 favoring short, dense favoring long, or vice-versa)? If so, a doc-length-adaptive fusion weight would be a lever. Cached BGE + stem/stop BM25, full-N:

| dataset (median doc len) | mean len of relevants caught **only-dense / only-BM25 / both** | score-vs-length **Spearman** BM25 / dense | recall@100 **short / long** half BM25 / dense |
|---|---|---|---|
| **scifact** (204w) | 239 / 210 / 230 (n=19/9/306) | **+0.011 / +0.023** | 0.922/0.933 · 0.960/0.950 |
| **nfcorpus** (237w) | 245 / 231 / 239 (n=968/623/1534) | **+0.007 / −0.010** | 0.255/0.236 · 0.285/0.312 |
| **arguana** (150w) | 97 / — / 133 (n=8/0/288) | **−0.041 / +0.031** | 0.949/1.000 · 0.986/1.000 |

**Findings:**
1. **Score-vs-length Spearman is ≈0 for BOTH tiers on ALL 3 corpora** (|ρ| ≤ 0.041; within each query's top-200 candidates). Neither default-`b` BM25 nor the unit-normalized dense cosine tier has a meaningful within-pool length preference. This is the robust headline.
2. **Relevants caught only-by-dense vs only-by-BM25 differ in length only marginally and INCONSISTENTLY** — dense-only slightly LONGER on scifact (+29w) and nfcorpus (+14w) but SHORTER on arguana (arguana BM25-only n=0, undefined; both tiers already catch the single relevant). No consistent length specialization.
3. **Length-stratified recall@100 shows NO stable crossover**: the weak short/long tilt flips SIGN by corpus — on the informative many-relevant nfcorpus dense is better on the long half (0.285→0.312) and BM25 on the short half (0.255→0.236), but on scifact it's the OPPOSITE (dense better short, BM25 better long). A sign-inconsistent, sub-0.03 effect = no exploitable rule.
4. **Rules out doc-length-adaptive fusion weighting** — the document-side analog of the already-rejected query-length-adaptive weighting (`e837e15`). BOTH length-adaptive fusion variants (query-length and doc-length) are now measured-rejected: length carries no differential tier signal to exploit. Also CONFIRMS default-`b`=0.75 BM25 is already length-neutral (ρ≈0, validating the `43be67e` finding that `b` controls length-norm — at default `b` the residual length correlation is gone) and BGE cosine is length-neutral in the 100–250w range these corpora span.

**Net:** document length is NOT a source of tier decorrelation — the hybrid's complementarity is vocabulary/semantic (`c26651f`), and both tiers are effectively length-blind after their respective normalizations (BM25's `b`, dense unit-norm). A measured negative that closes the doc-length-adaptive-fusion lever and sharpens the "why hybrid" mechanism to purely lexical-vs-semantic.
