Caveats: full-N; corpora span 150–237w medians (a corpus with extreme length variance or docs exceeding the embedder's 512-token window could differ — that is the separate truncation/chunking question, untested here); arguana length-analysis is uninformative (1-rel/q, recall-saturated). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `length_bias.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — RRF fusion POOL depth saturates at ~50 candidates/tier for nDCG@10: shipped POOL=100 is 2× deeper than needed, POOL≈50 is the free efficiency point, >100 is pure waste

Swept the RRF fusion pool depth — how many candidates/tier feed the reciprocal-rank sum before taking top-10 (a distinct knob from the *rerank* FEED `5129bf9` and per-tier `k` `516280a`, neither of which touched the fusion pool itself). BM25+contextual-BGE hybrid, full-N, nDCG@10, Δ vs shipped POOL=100:

| POOL | scifact | nfcorpus | arguana |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 10 | −0.0024 | −0.0050 | −0.0074 |
| 20 | −0.0039 | −0.0008 | −0.0016 |
| **50** | **+0.0010** | **+0.0016** | **−0.0004** |
| 100 (shipped) | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 |
| 200 | +0.0003 | +0.0002 | −0.0001 |
| 500 | +0.0013 | −0.0001 | −0.0001 |

(absolute nDCG@10 at POOL=50: scifact 0.7393, nfcorpus 0.3614, arguana 0.4199)

**Findings:**
1. **nDCG@10 SATURATES by POOL≈50 on all 3 corpora**: POOL=50 is within ±0.0016 of POOL=100/200/500 everywhere, and actually EQUALS-or-BEATS the shipped POOL=100 on scifact (+0.0010) and nfcorpus (+0.0016), essentially ties on arguana (−0.0004). **Deeper pools (200, 500) add nothing** (|Δ|≤0.0013, sign-inconsistent = noise).
2. **There IS a shallow floor**: POOL=10 costs −0.0024…−0.0074, POOL=20 costs −0.0008…−0.0039 — you need ~50 candidates/tier, not 10. A doc strongly ranked by one tier but ranked 20–50 by the other needs the pool to reach it to collect the cross-tier agreement bonus; below ~50 those get truncated out.
3. **Saturation holds EVEN on the recall-bound corpus**: nfcorpus is 38-rel/q and RECALL-bound (recall@100+ keeps climbing, `c039c3a`), yet its *nDCG@10* still saturates at POOL=50 — because nDCG@10 is top-heavy, the relevants that reach the final top-10 are found within each tier's first ~50 candidates. **Deeper fusion pools help recall@100+ but NOT ranking-at-10.** So the right POOL depends on the metric: ~50 for nDCG@10 / precision-at-k; deeper only if you optimize deep recall.
4. **Mechanism = RRF sharpness** (`561e8a5`): reciprocal-rank mass concentrates on top ranks — a rank-50 candidate contributes 1/(10+50)=0.017 vs 1/(10+1)=0.091 for rank-1 — so candidates past ~50 rarely accumulate enough to enter the top-10. This is the fusion-pool analog of why the *rerank* FEED also peaks at ~50 (`5129bf9`): both are governed by relevance concentrating in the top ~50, though they are different knobs (RRF sum vs cross-encoder feed).

**Net:** the shipped POOL=100 is SAFE but 2× deeper than necessary — **POOL≈50 gives equal-or-better nDCG@10 at half the per-tier candidate processing** (a free ~2× reduction in fusion + downstream feed work; not a quality win — the nDCG delta is ~0 — an efficiency finding). Don't go below ~50 (real floor) and don't bother above ~100 (pure waste) unless optimizing deep recall. Caveats: full-N, single shipped tier-weight (1.3/1.0), nDCG@10 metric (deep-recall metrics would want more). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `pool_depth.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — The hybrid's win is an AVERAGE property, not per-query dominance: fusion strictly beats the better single tier on only 6-13% of queries and LOSES on 22-44%; oracle per-query routing headroom is a large +0.04-0.06 — bounds all the rejected routing work

All prior findings report the hybrid's MEAN win and reject specific CHEAP routing signals (`e837e15` query-length weighting, `102a854` per-query rerank gating). Nobody measured the distributional picture or the oracle ceiling on ANY router. Per-query nDCG@10, BM25+contextual-BGE, full-N:

| dataset | BM25 | BGE | hybrid | oracle(BM25,BGE) per-q | oracle(+hybrid) | **route headroom** vs always-hybrid | **hybrid WIN/TIE/LOSE** vs best single | win mag / loss mag |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.6842 | 0.7203 | 0.7382 | 0.7864 | 0.7959 | **+0.0577** | 6% / 72% / 22% | +0.159 / −0.258 |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.3294 | 0.3382 | 0.3594 | 0.3905 | 0.3976 | **+0.0382** | 13% / 43% / 44% | +0.056 / −0.087 |
| **arguana** | 0.3460 | 0.4133 | 0.4200 | 0.4597 | 0.4659 | **+0.0459** | 7% / 56% / 37% | +0.093 / −0.124 |

**Findings:**
1. **Vs the best FIXED single tier (the real deployable alternative), the hybrid wins on all 3** (0.7382>BGE 0.7203, 0.3594>0.3382, 0.4200>0.4133) — hybrid-as-default is intact. But that mean win is MODEST (+0.007 to +0.021).
2. **Per-query, fusion is NOT a Pareto improvement**: it strictly beats the better single tier on only **6% / 13% / 7%** of queries (true synergy — a relevant doc neither tier top-ranked surfaces via cross-tier agreement), TIES on the saturated majority (72% / 43% / 56% — usually the ~1 relevant is already rank-1 in the better tier), and **LOSES on 22% / 44% / 37%** (fusion demotes a relevant the better tier had high, diluted by the weaker tier). On many-relevant nfcorpus the hybrid is WORSE than the best single tier on 44% of queries — yet its MEAN is still highest because *which* tier is best flips query-to-query and no FIXED single tier wins them all.
3. **Loss magnitudes EXCEED win magnitudes** (mean loss −0.087…−0.258 vs mean win +0.056…+0.159): when fusion hurts it hurts more than when it helps. The hybrid nets positive only because ties dominate and it never has to commit to the wrong fixed tier.
4. **Oracle per-query routing headroom over always-hybrid is a LARGE +0.038…+0.058** (8–13% relative) — a measured ceiling on ANY per-query router (even one allowed to keep the hybrid). This is the single largest retrieval-side headroom number in the arc, and it **unifies the rejected routing work**: the ceiling is real and big, but it's the classic UNREALIZABLE oracle — `e837e15`/`102a854` already showed no cheap query-time signal predicts the per-query winner. The gap between the +0.04–0.06 oracle and the ~0 achievable-by-cheap-signal is exactly why routing keeps looking tempting and keeps failing.

**Net:** the hybrid is the best FIXED policy (captures the cross-tier expectation without needing to predict the per-query winner), NOT a per-query optimum — it trades frequent small single-tier losses for never committing to the wrong tier. Fusion's irreducible synergy (beats BOTH tiers) is only 6–13% of queries; the rest of its mean win is robustness. The large oracle-router headroom (+0.04–0.06) reframes the remaining big lever as a *per-query tier/router predictor* (or a strong reranker that recovers the demoted relevants), consistent with "the reranker is the only place large gains remain" — but cheap query-features can't realize it (measured, `e837e15`/`102a854`). Design caution: the hybrid is not a per-query free lunch; expect it to under-perform a single tier on 20–44% of queries. Caveats: full-N, nDCG@10, oracle over {BM25,BGE,hybrid} only (adding the reranker as a 4th option would raise the ceiling further). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `oracle_route.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — The reranker's entire mean gain is a CONCENTRATED recovery of the fusion-LOSS tail: jina-turbo is positive ONLY on the queries where fusion demoted a relevant (recovers 30-41% of the gap, recall-capped), slightly NEGATIVE where fusion already won — unifies oracle-routing (`6cc008e`) + reranker-equalizer (`683a3d4`) + recall-ceiling (`c039c3a`)

Direct extension of the oracle-routing finding (`6cc008e`: fusion LOSES to the best single tier on 22-44% of queries; oracle routing headroom +0.04-0.06). Question: does a cross-encoder reranker recover those fusion-loss queries — i.e., realize the routing headroom by re-promoting the relevants fusion demoted? Partitioned queries by hybrid-vs-oracle-single and reranked the hybrid top-50 with jina-turbo (RRF-combine, `5129bf9`-optimal). Full-N, nDCG@10:

| dataset | hybrid | reranked | Δrerank (overall) | oracle-single | reranked − oracle | **LOSS%** | **LOSS: rerank recovers / of gap** | TIE Δ | WIN Δ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|---:|---:|
| **scifact** | 0.7382 | 0.7526 | **+0.0144** | 0.7864 | −0.0338 | 22% | **+0.0776 / +0.2584 (30%)** | −0.0035 | −0.0082 |
| **nfcorpus** | 0.3594 | 0.3703 | **+0.0108** | 0.3905 | −0.0202 | 44% | **+0.0353 / +0.0868 (41%)** | −0.0073 | −0.0120 |
| **arguana** | 0.4200 | 0.4315 | **+0.0115** | 0.4597 | −0.0282 | 37% | **+0.0483 / +0.1241 (39%)** | −0.0097 | −0.0132 |

**Findings:**
1. **The reranker's ENTIRE overall gain comes from the fusion-LOSS partition.** On every dataset rerank is strongly positive on the LOSS queries (+0.0776 / +0.0353 / +0.0483) and consistently slightly NEGATIVE on both the TIE partition (−0.0035…−0.0097) and the WIN partition (−0.0082…−0.0132). The cross-encoder is a **targeted fusion-loss-recovery mechanism**: it earns its keep precisely on the queries where fusion demoted a relevant the better single tier had ranked high, and it mildly perturbs the rankings fusion already got right. The modest ~+0.01 MEAN massively understates a concentrated +0.04–0.08 lift on the 22–44% loss tail.
2. **Rerank recovers only 30–41% of the loss gap — a MINORITY.** The demoted relevants it can re-promote are those still in the top-50 feed; the rest were pushed out of the pool by the fusion and are unrecoverable. This is the recall-ceiling (`683a3d4`/`6911260`/`c039c3a`) made concrete on the exact queries that need recovery: the reranker equalizes RANKING up to preserved recall, never recall itself.
3. **Reranked-hybrid never reaches the oracle-single ceiling** (−0.0202…−0.0338 short on all 3). So the reranker realizes only PART of the +0.04–0.06 oracle routing headroom (`6cc008e`); the residual (−0.02…−0.034) is fusion-demoted relevants that fell out of the feed — closable only by better RECALL (deeper feed / better retrieval) or a true per-query router, NOT by more reranking.
4. **Validates rerank-ALWAYS over per-query gating** (`102a854`): even though rerank slightly hurts the tie/win partitions, the loss-partition gain (+0.035…+0.078) × loss-fraction (22–44%) dwarfs the tie/win losses (−0.003…−0.013), so the net is positive on all 3. You would only skip rerank on tie/win queries if a cheap signal could identify them — and `102a854`/`e837e15` already showed it can't. So rerank everything; its value is concentrated but its harm where inert is negligible.

**Net:** the reranker is not a diffuse quality boost — it is a **surgical recovery of the fusion-loss tail**, active on exactly the 22–44% of queries where fusion's tier-mixing demoted a relevant, near-inert elsewhere, and capped at ~1/3 of the loss gap by feed recall. This mechanistically explains the reranker's small-but-real mean gain and ties together three threads: it partially realizes the oracle-routing headroom (`6cc008e`), it does so as a ranking-equalizer bounded by recall (`683a3d4`), and the residual gap is pure recall loss (`c039c3a`). To capture MORE of the headroom, spend on RECALL (deeper feed / stronger retrieval) — the reranker is already recall-saturated on the queries that matter. Caveats: full-N, nDCG@10, FEED=50, RRF-combine, jina-turbo, oracle-single = per-query max(BM25,BGE). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop + `jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en`, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `rerank_recover.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Union/pre-fusion candidate feed does NOT help the reranker (demoted relevants are already 100% in the fused top-50 feed) — and this CORRECTS `2a90c06`: the reranked-vs-oracle-single residual is a CROSS-ENCODER ranking limit, NOT a feed-recall deficit

`2a90c06` showed the reranker recovers only 30–41% of the fusion-loss gap and I attributed the residual to the recall-ceiling ("only demoted relevants still in the top-50 feed are recoverable"). Natural lever: feed the reranker the UNION of per-tier top-50 (which preserves each tier's high-ranked relevants *before* fusion demotes them) instead of the FUSED top-50. Tested the precondition cheaply (cached, no reranker) — does the union feed actually contain relevants the fused feed misses, especially the demoted ones? Full-N:

| dataset (loss%) | feed size fused / union | LOSS-q recall in feed fused / union | **DEMOTED relevants present in feed** fused / union (n) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **scifact** (22%) | 50 / 85 | 0.993 / 0.993 (Δ+0.000) | **1.000 / 1.000** (n=10) |
| **nfcorpus** (44%) | 50 / 88 | 0.279 / 0.319 (Δ+0.040) | **1.000 / 1.000** (n=111) |
| **arguana** (37%) | 50 / 76 | 1.000 / 1.000 (Δ+0.000) | **1.000 / 1.000** (n=11) |

(DEMOTED = relevants the best-single tier ranked in its top-10 but the hybrid dropped from its top-10 — the docs that CAUSE the fusion loss.)

**Findings:**
1. **The demoted relevants are ALREADY in the fused top-50 feed 100% of the time on all 3 corpora.** A relevant that the best single tier ranks in its top-10 gets a large RRF contribution and lands in the fused top-50 — just at rank ~15–40, not the top-10 — even when the other tier ranks it low. So the reranker HAD every loss-causing relevant in its candidate set already.
2. **Union / pre-fusion feed is REJECTED as a lever**: on the LOSS partition it contains no more of the demoted relevants than the fused feed (Δ demoted = 0 on all 3). Its only extra content is deep-tail OTHER relevants (nfcorpus all-recall +0.036, the ONE many-relevant corpus) that NEITHER tier top-ranked — irrelevant to closing the loss gap — bought at +50–76% more rerank cost (feed 76–88 vs 50). No benefit where it matters.
3. **CORRECTION to `2a90c06`**: the reranked-hybrid-vs-oracle-single residual (−0.020…−0.034) is NOT a feed-recall deficit — the relevants are present in the feed. It is the **cross-encoder's own ranking limitation**: jina-turbo has the demoted relevant in hand and scores it too low to reach the top-10 (recovering only 30–41% of the gap). "Recall-capped" was the wrong mechanism for THIS residual; the correct one is CE-scoring-capped. (The separate ABSOLUTE nDCG ceiling on nfcorpus — all-q feed recall only 0.27–0.30, 70% of relevants beyond any top-50 — IS genuinely recall-bound, but that caps the oracle-single itself, a different gap.)
4. **Redirects the `2a90c06` "spend on recall" takeaway**: to close the reranked-vs-oracle-single residual you need a BETTER CROSS-ENCODER (or to accept it), NOT a smarter/deeper feed — the feed already contains the answers. Deeper/union feeding only helps the deep-recall ceiling on many-relevant corpora, a distinct objective (r@100+, `c039c3a`).

**Net:** the intuitive "feed the reranker pre-fusion candidates so it can undo the demotion" lever is a measured dead end — fusion never actually removes the demoted relevants from the top-50, it just under-ranks them, and the reranker already sees them. The remaining fusion-loss residual is bounded by cross-encoder ranking quality, not candidate availability. This tightens the mechanism from `2a90c06` and closes the feed-source question. Caveats: full-N; "demoted" defined via best-single top-10 vs hybrid top-10; union = BM25-top-50 ∪ BGE-top-50. No reranker run needed — the precondition (relevants-in-feed) is decisive. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `feed_recall.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CobaltRidge — `blend_two_tier_aligned_unique` is NOT a 10k current-main win; only wire it for the measured 100k `limit_all` shape

Tested the tempting follow-up to `blend_two_tier_aligned`: since vector-index fast hits are unique by doc id, skip the
aligned blend's defensive `AHashMap<&str, ScorePair>` merge and stream directly into the output vector. Same-binary
`blend_aligned` bench, local fallback after RCH worker `vmi1167313` exceeded the 10-minute bench cutoff:

| N | current main `aligned` | `aligned_unique` | ratio |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 10000 | 2.2163 ms `[1.8569,2.7936]` | 2.2917 ms `[1.9936,2.5115]` | **1.034** |
| 100000 | 67.779 ms `[52.004,83.726]` | 34.245 ms `[30.626,37.913]` | **0.505** |

**Decision then:** reject the 10k specialization as a standalone lever. Although it is still faster than the older
materialized ORIG at 10k (2.2917 ms vs 2.6086 ms, ratio 0.878), current main already has the aligned clone-elision win,
and the unique path is not better at 10k. Production was therefore thresholded at 100k. This local-fallback result is now
superseded by the same-worker RCH measurement below.

### 2026-07-04 — CobaltRidge — same-worker RCH overturns the noisy 10k unique-blend reject; threshold lowered to 10k

The required literal command was tried first and rejected by Cargo (`unexpected argument '--release'`):

```bash
AGENT_NAME=CobaltRidge CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/search-cod \
  rch exec -- cargo bench --release -p frankensearch-fusion --bench blend_aligned -- \
  blend_aligned --sample-size 10 --warm-up-time 1 --measurement-time 1
```

Successful per-crate bench, same remote worker `ovh-a`:

```bash
AGENT_NAME=CobaltRidge CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/data/projects/.rch-targets/search-cod \
  rch exec -- cargo bench -p frankensearch-fusion --profile release --bench blend_aligned -- \
  blend_aligned --sample-size 10 --warm-up-time 1 --measurement-time 1
```

| N | legacy materialized ORIG `current` | current main `aligned` | `aligned_unique` | ratio vs current main | ratio vs ORIG | Decision |
|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---|
| 10000 | 1.0478 ms `[1.0250,1.0940]` | 1.1052 ms `[1.0767,1.1456]` | 874.55 µs `[868.76,882.15]` | **0.791** | **0.835** | wire |
| 50000 | 10.876 ms `[10.721,11.097]` | 10.986 ms `[9.9437,12.771]` | 5.5231 ms `[5.3568,5.6369]` | **0.503** | **0.508** | wire |
| 100000 | 31.353 ms `[24.605,36.328]` | 23.653 ms `[22.331,25.078]` | 12.708 ms `[12.015,14.291]` | **0.537** | **0.405** | already wired |

**Decision now:** the 10k no-ship was measurement noise from a local fallback. Lower
`ALIGNED_UNIQUE_BLEND_MIN_HITS` from 100k to 10k and keep the 50k bench arm so the crossover remains covered. This is a
measured ratio-vs-ORIG entry for the BOLD `limit_all` result-assembly surface; external Tantivy/Lucene/Meili ratio is not
claimed because the bench isolates frankensearch's internal blend/materialization region.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Abstention/confidence is corpus-specific, NOT universal: the cross-encoder top score is the best "is there a good answer" signal on CLEAN-relevance corpora (AUROC ~0.78) but COLLAPSES on argumentative retrieval, and NO signal (CE or retrieval) supports a corpus-agnostic global threshold

Novel angle (no prior finding touches calibration/abstention): can the system tell when it has NO good answer? Cross-encoder scores are ABSOLUTE relevance judgments (unlike BM25/cosine, only relative within a query), so the top CE score SHOULD be a corpus-agnostic confidence signal where retrieval scores fail. Tested: per query, predict hit@10 (any relevant in top-10) from the top jina-turbo CE score vs retrieval signals (top fused score, fused score-gap, tier top-10 agreement, max BM25, max cosine). AUROC, full-N:

| signal | scifact (hit 0.87) | nfcorpus (0.73) | arguana (0.79) | **POOLED (global threshold)** |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **top1_ce** | **0.789** | **0.774** | 0.582 | 0.640 |
| top1_fused | 0.753 | 0.721 | 0.449 | 0.621 |
| gap_fused | 0.748 | 0.690 | 0.433 | 0.645 |
| agree (BM25∩BGE top10) | 0.657 | 0.765 | 0.594 | **0.666** |
| max_bm25 | 0.729 | 0.741 | 0.395 | 0.599 |
| max_cos | 0.731 | 0.722 | 0.581 | 0.614 |

**Findings:**
1. **Within a CLEAN-relevance corpus, the CE top score is the best abstention signal** — scifact (factual claims) 0.789 and nfcorpus (medical) 0.774, beating every retrieval signal. So the cross-encoder's absolute score IS a usable per-query "is there a relevant doc in the results" confidence signal for factual/topical retrieval, and PER-CORPUS abstention thresholding is viable there.
2. **It COLLAPSES on argumentative retrieval** (arguana 0.582, barely above chance) — and there ALL signals fail, with the retrieval scores going WORSE than random (top1_fused 0.449, max_bm25 0.395, i.e. high retrieval confidence ANTI-predicts a good answer). Mechanism: arguana relevance = "correct COUNTER-argument stance," which is invisible to both retrieval similarity and the CE's topical-relevance score — a confident high score is often a topically-similar WRONG-STANCE doc (the argument being countered). Confidence signals fail on stance/argument tasks.
3. **POOLED (a single corpus-agnostic threshold), NO signal supports reliable abstention** — all AUROC 0.60–0.67, barely above chance, and the CE score (0.640) is NOT better than cheap tier-agreement (0.666) or the fused-score gap (0.645). The CE's theoretical advantage (absolute, cross-query-comparable scores) does NOT yield a corpus-agnostic threshold: jina-turbo's calibration is corpus-specific (its arguana failure drags the pool down). My hypothesis "CE scores are absolute → universal confidence signal" is empirically REJECTED.
4. **Degree-of-quality wants a different signal than binary abstention**: for continuous Spearman-with-nDCG the fused-score GAP is the best pooled predictor (0.295) while the CE score is weak pooled (0.131) — the CE score separates has-answer/no-answer within clean corpora but doesn't rank quality across them.

**Net:** abstention is achievable but CORPUS-SPECIFIC — use the CE top score with a PER-CORPUS threshold for factual/topical retrieval (AUROC ~0.78, better than any retrieval signal); expect it to fail on argumentative/stance corpora, and expect NO global threshold to generalize (all signals ~0.64 pooled). The intuition that cross-encoder absolute scores give a universal confidence gauge does not hold across corpus types; calibration must be fit per domain. Ties to `b0a409a` (arguana is the hardest corpus for rerankers) and the recall/difficulty structure. Caveats: full-N, hit@10 binary target, jina-turbo (a small distilled reranker — a larger CE might calibrate better across corpora), single global threshold via pooled AUROC. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop + `jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en`, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `abstain.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — The hybrid's margin over the best single tier is corpus-size-INVARIANT (does NOT grow with distractors): fusion degrades in lockstep with single tiers, it is not a distractor-protection mechanism at scale — so the arc's hybrid-advantage findings EXTRAPOLATE

The whole arc is measured on tiny 3.6–8.7k-doc corpora; real corpora are far larger. Hypothesis: because the two tiers make DECORRELATED mistakes, fusion should filter distractors better as they multiply → the hybrid's margin should GROW with corpus size (meaning the arc UNDERSTATES production value). Tested by keeping all relevants and subsampling non-relevant distractors to hit target sizes; refit BM25, sliced cached BGE. nDCG@10:

| corpus | size (small→full) | BM25 | BGE | hybrid | best-single | **margin (hybrid − best)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **scifact** (0.9 rel/q) | 518 | 0.844 | 0.874 | 0.888 | 0.874 | +0.0144 |
| | 1296 | 0.804 | 0.814 | 0.838 | 0.814 | +0.0241 |
| | 2592 | 0.765 | 0.772 | 0.792 | 0.772 | +0.0197 |
| | 5183 | 0.684 | 0.720 | 0.738 | 0.720 | +0.0180 |
| **arguana** (1.0 rel/q) | 867 | 0.698 | 0.758 | 0.759 | 0.758 | +0.0016 |
| | 2168 | 0.623 | 0.683 | 0.696 | 0.683 | +0.0124 |
| | 4337 | 0.487 | 0.574 | 0.574 | 0.574 | +0.0003 |
| | 8674 | 0.346 | 0.413 | 0.420 | 0.413 | +0.0067 |

**Findings:**
1. **All tiers degrade sharply as distractors multiply** — over ~10× more docs, scifact BGE 0.874→0.720, BM25 0.844→0.684; arguana BGE 0.758→0.413, BM25 0.698→0.346. The experiment bites (distractors genuinely make retrieval harder), and arguana's difficulty is heavily DISTRACTOR-driven (biggest collapse — long argumentative docs are highly confusable).
2. **The hybrid's MARGIN over the best single tier is size-INVARIANT — it does NOT grow with corpus size.** scifact holds ~+0.018–0.024 across a 10× range; arguana is small and noisy (~+0.005 avg). The hybrid degrades in LOCKSTEP with the single tiers → **fusion is NOT a distractor-protection mechanism at scale**; its advantage is a fixed relative lift (~+2–3% relative on scifact, ~+1% arguana), not a growing one. HYPOTHESIS REJECTED.
3. **Weak floor effect at tiny corpus sizes**: at the smallest size the margin is lowest (scifact +0.0144 @518, arguana +0.0016 @867) because the single tiers are near-ceiling (nDCG 0.76–0.87) and fusion is redundant; the margin rises to its plateau once the task is hard enough that single tiers make mistakes, then stays flat.
4. **Extrapolation-validity payoff**: because the margin is size-invariant across 10×, the arc's hybrid-advantage findings EXTRAPOLATE — the ~+0.02 margin is a stable property of tier DECORRELATION, neither understated nor overstated by the small BEIR corpora. (Caveat: only a 10× range is testable here; ≥100× is unmeasured.)
5. **Methodological caveat**: **nfcorpus is UNUSABLE for distractor-scaling** — 86% of its docs (3128/3633) are relevant to some query, so you cannot subsample below the relevant set (all sub-full fractions collapse to the same 3128-doc subset). Anyone doing corpus-scaling on BEIR should skip nfcorpus.

**Net:** fusion buys a size-INVARIANT ~+0.02 nDCG margin, not a scale-amplified one — the two tiers' decorrelated errors give a constant relative edge that neither compounds nor washes out as distractors grow (10× tested). Good news for extrapolation (the arc's findings hold at larger scale), bad news for anyone hoping fusion is a distractor-robustness lever (it isn't; all tiers sink together). Caveats: 10× size range only, seeded distractor subsample, best-single = per-corpus max(BM25,BGE) which is always BGE here. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/arguana (nfcorpus excluded — too dense), full test sets, no cargo/torch. `corpus_scale.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Max-passage pooling does NOT beat whole-doc dense retrieval for short docs: net-negative (catastrophic −0.082 on argumentative arguana), helps only heterogeneous-localized nfcorpus, and the hybrid absorbs even that — don't chunk short/holistic documents

The long-doc chunking question, approached via the tractable *pooling* sub-question: does splitting a doc into sentences and MAX-pooling the query-sentence cosine (the standard passage-retrieval technique) beat a single WHOLE-DOC embedding, which averages/dilutes a locally-relevant sentence? Isolated with the same fast static embedder (model2vec potion-retrieval-32M) at sentence vs doc granularity, full-N, nDCG@10:

| corpus (sents/doc) | dense whole-doc | dense max-passage | **Δ dense** | hybrid BM25+whole | hybrid BM25+maxpass | **Δ hybrid** |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (9.8) | 0.6345 | 0.6198 | **−0.0147** | 0.7043 | 0.6906 | −0.0137 |
| **nfcorpus** (11.0) | 0.3087 | 0.3206 | **+0.0119** | 0.3393 | 0.3381 | −0.0012 |
| **arguana** (7.7) | 0.3186 | 0.2366 | **−0.0820** | 0.3490 | 0.3303 | −0.0187 |

**Findings:**
1. **Max-passage pooling is net-NEGATIVE for the dense tier on 2/3** (scifact −0.0147, arguana −0.0820) and only marginally positive on nfcorpus (+0.0119). In the HYBRID it is negative on all 3 (−0.0137 / −0.0012 / −0.0187). The intuitive "chunk docs so the query can match a specific passage" LOSES for these abstract-length documents.
2. **arguana is catastrophic (−0.0820 dense)**: argument / counter-argument relevance is HOLISTIC — it depends on the whole argument's STANCE, not a single sentence. Max-passage selects the single most-query-similar sentence, which for counter-argument retrieval is exactly the WRONG signal (a query-similar sentence restates the query's position; it does not counter it). Whole-doc averaging preserves the holistic stance signal that max destroys. (Same holistic-relevance failure mode that broke confidence signals on arguana, `4c2d358`.)
3. **Mechanism — max-passage helps ONLY when relevance is LOCALIZED and the doc is topically HETEROGENEOUS** (nfcorpus: 11 sents/doc, multi-subtopic medical, +0.012 dense) — there a query targeting one sub-topic benefits from not being diluted by the rest. It HURTS for short FOCUSED docs where whole-doc averaging is beneficial CONTEXT aggregation (scifact claims with qualifiers) or where relevance is HOLISTIC (arguana stance). The max operator is also high-variance: one spuriously-query-similar sentence dominates the doc's score.
4. **Even nfcorpus's dense gain (+0.012) VANISHES in the hybrid (−0.001)** — BM25's exact term-matching already captures the localized-passage signal, so passage pooling is REDUNDANT with the lexical tier for local-match queries. The hybrid never benefits from max-passage on any corpus.

**Net:** for short focused documents (abstracts, claims, arguments — the common IR case), WHOLE-DOC dense embedding beats max-passage pooling; do NOT chunk short/holistic docs. Passage-level indexing pays only for LONG, topically-heterogeneous documents with LOCALIZED relevance, and even there the lexical tier captures much of the same signal in a hybrid. This partially answers the long-doc chunking question by identifying WHEN chunking helps (heterogeneous + localized) vs hurts (holistic + short). Caveats: static bag-of-words embedder (potion) — but the mechanism (max selects the single most-similar sentence, wrong for holistic relevance; whole-doc aggregates context) is embedder-independent and likely STRENGTHENS for a contextual embedder (attention aggregates context better, and max-noise remains); pure MAX pooling (softer mean-of-top-M might reduce variance — untested route-next); true long-doc (>512 tok) regime needs a long-doc corpus to test the localized-relevance case properly. Verified: `model2vec` potion-retrieval-32M + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `passage_pool.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Pooling closure: mean-top-M is strictly rejected; a whole-doc+max BLEND recovers the localized signal (dense +0.03 on focused/heterogeneous corpora, avoids pure-max's catastrophe) but the HYBRID absorbs it — whole-doc pooling stays right for the shipped system

Follow-up to the max-passage rejection (`d919aed`, pure max net-negative & high-variance). Tested the flagged softer/blended variants: mean-of-top-M sentences, and a whole-doc + λ·max blend (holistic anchor + localized boost). potion, full-N, nDCG@10, Δ vs whole-doc:

| variant | scifact dense / hybrid | nfcorpus dense / hybrid | arguana dense / hybrid |
|---|---|---|---|
| max | −0.0147 / −0.0137 | +0.0119 / −0.0012 | **−0.0820** / −0.0187 |
| mean-top3 | −0.0212 / −0.0110 | +0.0195 / +0.0043 | −0.0879 / −0.0294 |
| mean-top5 | −0.0496 / −0.0213 | +0.0059 / +0.0003 | −0.1192 / −0.0435 |
| **blend +0.5·max** | **+0.0284** / −0.0029 | +0.0217 / **+0.0090** | −0.0089 / −0.0059 |
| **blend +1.0·max** | **+0.0328** / −0.0057 | **+0.0277** / **+0.0106** | −0.0195 / −0.0068 |

**Findings:**
1. **mean-top-M pooling is strictly REJECTED** — uniformly ≤ whole-doc on the dense tier (worse than even pure max on scifact −0.021/−0.050 and arguana −0.088/−0.119); averaging more sentences just DILUTES further, it does not fix max's problem. Softening the pool is the wrong direction.
2. **The whole-doc + max BLEND recovers the localized signal without pure-max's catastrophe.** Big dense gains on scifact (+0.033) and nfcorpus (+0.028) — the whole-doc component anchors holistic relevance while the max term adds a localized boost — and it CONVERTS pure-max's −0.082 arguana disaster into only −0.020 (the whole-doc anchor dominates). So blending is strictly better than pure max as a dense representation, and a modest real WIN for a DENSE-ONLY system on focused/heterogeneous corpora.
3. **But the blend is corpus-dependent and the HYBRID absorbs it.** In the shipped hybrid, blend helps ONLY nfcorpus (+0.011), is ~0 on scifact (−0.006) and slightly negative on arguana (−0.007). BM25's exact term-matching already supplies the localized-passage signal the max term adds, so the standalone-dense blend gain (+0.03) does NOT survive fusion — except on nfcorpus (heterogeneous multi-subtopic medical, many relevants), where the localized max signal adds recall the lexical tier misses.
4. **Same lexical-absorption theme as `43be67e` (BM25 b-tuning) and `19787ed` (title-boost)**: a dense-side micro-optimization that clearly helps the standalone dense tier is ABSORBED by the lexical tier in the hybrid, because the vector tier's value in the fusion is decorrelated recall, not stacked precision tricks the lexical tier already covers.

**Net (pooling/granularity question CLOSED):** whole-doc pooling is correct for the shipped HYBRID — the only pooling that beats it (a whole-doc-anchored + max blend) is absorbed by the lexical tier (nfcorpus aside). A whole-doc+max blend IS a modest win (+0.03 dense) for a DENSE-ONLY system on focused/heterogeneous corpora and safe-ish on holistic ones (−0.02 vs pure-max −0.08); mean-top-M and pure-max are both rejected. Ranking of dense poolings: **blend > whole-doc > max > mean-top-M**. Caveats: static embedder (potion); blend λ untuned per-corpus (label-free λ=0.5–1.0 all positive on the 2 non-holistic corpora); arguana's holistic relevance penalizes any localized term. Verified: `model2vec` potion-retrieval-32M + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana full test sets, no cargo/torch. `pool_variants.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — External validity: the structural findings GENERALIZE to a 4th dataset (SciDocs, 25.6k docs, LEXICAL-dominant regime) — hybrid-beats-single, per-query oracle-routing (WIN 10%/TIE 53%/LOSE 37%, headroom +0.037), and pool-depth ~50 saturation all reproduce

The arc's mechanistic findings live on 3 small corpora where the DENSE tier leads (scifact/arguana). Biggest external-validity risk: do they hold on a larger corpus in the OPPOSITE (lexical-dominant) regime? Re-ran the key STRUCTURAL findings on SciDocs (25,657 docs — 3–7× larger than the original 3; 1000 queries; citation relevance, avg 4.9 rel/q) with potion + stem/stop BM25:

**1) Hybrid-beats-single generalizes to the LEXICAL-dominant regime** (`de979c7`/`9090074`):
- BM25 **0.1572** > dense 0.1368 (BM25 is the STRONGER tier here — confirms SciDocs is lexical-dominant) → hybrid **0.1603**, +0.0031 over the best single tier. The hybrid still wins even when the LEXICAL tier leads (opposite of scifact/arguana). Hybrid-as-default is REGIME-INDEPENDENT; its value doesn't depend on which modality is stronger — only on the decorrelation between them.

**2) Per-query oracle-routing structure reproduces exactly** (`6cc008e`):
- oracle-single 0.1920, oracle+hybrid 0.1974 → **route-headroom over always-hybrid +0.0370**.
- **hybrid WIN 10% / TIE 53% / LOSE 37%** vs the best single tier.
- Both numbers fall SQUARELY in the original 3-dataset ranges (WIN 6–13%, LOSE 22–44%; headroom +0.038–0.058). The core finding — the hybrid's win is an AVERAGE property, not per-query dominance, with a large UNREALIZABLE oracle-routing headroom — holds on a 4th, larger, opposite-regime dataset.

**3) Pool-depth saturation reproduces** (`ebb3377`):
- POOL 20 / 50 / 100 / 200 → Δ vs POOL=100 = −0.0004 / +0.0007 / 0 / +0.0001. nDCG@10 saturates by POOL≈20–50 (even shallower than the original ~50); POOL=100 is again 2× deeper than needed.

**Findings:**
1. **All three structural findings generalize** to SciDocs — a corpus 3–7× larger and in the opposite tier-dominance regime — with the per-query win/lose fractions (10%/37%) and routing headroom (+0.037) landing inside the original ranges, and pool saturation reproducing at ~50. Strong external-validity evidence: these are STRUCTURAL properties of RRF hybrid + BM25/dense decorrelation, not artifacts of the 3 small dense-dominant corpora.
2. **The lexical-dominant regime is the informative stress-test**: on SciDocs BM25 leads, so "up-weight the stronger tier" up-weighted BM25 — and the hybrid STILL beat it (+0.0031), reinforcing that fusion's value is the SECOND tier's decorrelated recall regardless of which tier is stronger (`72b68de`).
3. **Absolute nDCG is low (~0.16)** because SciDocs citation-prediction is intrinsically hard AND the static potion embedder is weak (the original mechanistic numbers used contextual BGE) — but the STRUCTURE (average-win / per-query-loss-fraction / routing-headroom / pool-saturation) is embedder-agnostic and is what generalizes; magnitudes would rise with a contextual embedder but the structural claims are what this validates.

**Net:** the arc's central structural findings (hybrid-as-default across regimes, the average-not-per-query nature of the hybrid win with large unrealizable oracle-routing headroom, and ~50-candidate pool saturation) are NOT specific to the 3 original corpora — they reproduce on a 4th, larger, lexical-dominant dataset. This closes the "do these hold beyond 3 datasets?" external-validity question for the structural results. Caveats: static embedder (structure not magnitude validated), full 1000-query test set, single potion run. Verified: `model2vec` potion-retrieval-32M + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR SciDocs full test set, no cargo/torch. `scidocs_struct.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Contextual BGE FLIPS SciDocs from lexical-dominant to DENSE-dominant (dense +45% vs static): tier dominance is EMBEDDER-determined, not corpus-intrinsic — and the per-query oracle-routing structure reproduces with the REAL contextual embedder (upgrades last turn's static-only validation)

Last turn (`6f1d554`) validated the structural findings on SciDocs but with the WEAK static potion embedder (caveat: structure-not-magnitude; SciDocs was lexical-dominant there, BM25>dense). Re-ran with the CONTEXTUAL embedder (fastembed BGE-small, freshly embedded all 25.6k docs — cache now persisted), N=1000:

| | BM25 | dense | hybrid | regime |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---|
| **static potion** (`6f1d554`) | 0.1572 | 0.1368 | 0.1603 | LEXICAL-dominant (BM25 leads) |
| **contextual BGE** (this) | 0.1572 | **0.1988** | 0.2052 | **DENSE-dominant (BGE flipped it)** |

**Findings:**
1. **The contextual embedder FLIPS the tier-dominance regime.** Static potion: BM25 0.1572 > dense 0.1368 (lexical-dominant). Contextual BGE: dense **0.1988** > BM25 0.1572 (dense-dominant). BGE lifts the dense tier **+45%** (0.1368→0.1988) while BM25 is unchanged (0.1572 — same stem/stop analyzer). So **which tier is stronger is EMBEDDER-determined, not a corpus-intrinsic property**: SciDocs citation-relevance only LOOKED lexical because the static embedder was too weak to capture it. Generalizes embedder-gating (`9090074`) to a 4th dataset, and RETRACTS the "SciDocs is a lexical-dominant corpus" framing — it's lexical-dominant only under a weak embedder.
2. **Hybrid-as-default holds with the strong embedder** — hybrid 0.2052 > best-single BGE 0.1988 (+0.0065), a modest in-range margin, consistent with the arc.
3. **Per-query oracle-routing reproduces with the REAL contextual embedder** (upgrades `6f1d554`'s static-only validation): WIN **17%** / TIE **43%** / LOSE **39%**, oracle-routing headroom **+0.0378** — squarely inside the original 3-dataset ranges (WIN 6–13%, LOSE 22–44%, headroom +0.038–0.058). So the "hybrid win is an AVERAGE property, not per-query dominance, with a large unrealizable routing headroom" (`6cc008e`) holds on a 4th dataset with the actual contextual embedder, not just the static proxy.
4. **The routing headroom is nearly IDENTICAL across embedders** (+0.0370 potion, +0.0378 BGE) despite the dense tier being +45% stronger under BGE — the unrealizable oracle-routing gap is a STRUCTURAL property of RRF fusion, not an artifact of embedder strength. (The WIN fraction rises 10%→17% as the dense tier strengthens — a stronger second tier finds more true cross-tier-synergy queries — but the overall structure is invariant.)

**Net:** contextual BGE flips SciDocs to dense-dominant, proving tier dominance is embedder-determined not corpus-intrinsic (no corpus is inherently "lexical" — only relative to embedder strength); the hybrid still wins; and the per-query oracle-routing structure reproduces on a 4th dataset with the REAL contextual embedder, upgrading last turn's static-only validation to full magnitude. External validity for the contextual mechanistic findings is now confirmed on a larger, independent corpus. Caches `scidocs_bgeC.npy`/`scidocs_bgeQ.npy` (persisted for future contextual work on a 4th dataset). Caveats: BGE-small (same as arc); single run; corpus embed took ~44min (25.6k docs). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR SciDocs full test set, no cargo/torch. `scidocs_bge.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — RRF + best-tier-rank BONUS (rescue fusion-demoted relevants) does NOT beat plain RRF-sum: mean ~+0.001 across 4 datasets, corpus-dependent (best +0.0038 arguana, worst −0.0009 scifact), sign tracks tier-imbalance — the fusion-loss residual is NOT recoverable at the fusion level

Directly motivated by the oracle-routing finding (`6cc008e`: fusion LOSES on 22–44% of queries because RRF-sum DEMOTES a relevant one tier ranked #1). Targeted fix: add a best-tier-rank BONUS to standard RRF — `score = RRF_sum + λ·max(per-tier reciprocal rank)` — rewarding top-in-EITHER-tier to rescue the demoted relevants, without discarding the consensus sum. Rank-based, cached, contextual BGE, all 4 datasets (incl. the new SciDocs BGE cache), nDCG@10, Δ vs plain RRF (λ=0):

| dataset (BM25 / BGE) | λ=0.25 | λ=0.5 | λ=1.0 | best Δ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (0.684 / 0.720) | −0.0009 | −0.0004 | −0.0005 | −0.0004 (λ=0 best) |
| **nfcorpus** (0.329 / 0.338) | +0.0005 | −0.0004 | −0.0013 | +0.0005 |
| **arguana** (0.346 / 0.413) | **+0.0038** | +0.0020 | +0.0032 | +0.0038 |
| **scidocs** (0.157 / 0.199) | +0.0006 | +0.0006 | +0.0003 | +0.0006 |

**Findings:**
1. **The best-tier-rank bonus is a WASH** — mean Δ ≈ +0.0010 at λ=0.25, 3/4 marginally positive but 1 negative, all within noise. Plain RRF-sum (λ=0) is effectively optimal on all 4 datasets. The rank-based additive rescue does not deliver the recovery the oracle finding motivated.
2. **Why it fails**: rewarding one-tier-top docs promotes one-tier-top FALSE POSITIVES symmetrically with the demoted relevants, and erodes the CONSENSUS advantage that fusion relies on for its wins (`6cc008e` showed fusion beats both tiers via consensus on the 6–13% synergy queries). The demotion the oracle finding identified CANNOT be cheaply undone at the fusion level — up-weighting best-tier-rank trades rescued relevants for promoted false positives ~evenly.
3. **Sign tracks tier IMBALANCE**: positive where ONE tier strongly dominates (arguana BGE 0.413 ≫ BM25 0.346 → +0.0038; scidocs BGE 0.199 > 0.157 → +0.0006), negative/neutral where the tiers are balanced (scifact 0.720/0.684, nfcorpus 0.338/0.329). Mechanism: when a tier dominates, its top docs are more reliably relevant so rewarding them helps; when balanced, the bonus also rewards the weaker tier's false positives. But the effect is tiny (+0.0038 ceiling) and λ would need per-corpus tuning against the imbalance (labels) → not a practical default.
4. **Ties to CombMAX (`RRF beats CombMAX`) and score-rescue (`6f89b5e`) rejections**: this is the RANK-based additive variant and it reaches the same conclusion — RRF-sum's consensus averaging is the correct fusion primitive; targeted one-tier rescue does not pay.

**Net:** the fusion-loss residual identified by the oracle finding (`6cc008e`) is NOT recoverable by a fusion-level best-tier-rank bonus — plain RRF-sum stays optimal, and the ONLY mechanism that touches the loss tail is the cross-encoder reranker (`2a90c06`, and only ~1/3 of it, CE-capped). This reinforces that the +0.04–0.06 oracle-routing headroom is genuinely hard: not cheaply exploitable at the retrieval level (`e837e15`/`102a854`), the fusion level (here), OR fully by reranking (`2a90c06`/`77ab5aa`). Caveat: λ untuned per-corpus (sign tracks imbalance, would need labels); rank-based on POOL=100. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana/scidocs full test sets, no cargo/torch. `maxrank.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — ★ WIN: kNN neighbor smoothing of dense scores helps the HYBRID on all 4 datasets (+0.004 mean, up to +0.007) — a RECALL mechanism that SURVIVES fusion, unlike the absorbed precision tricks; nearly free if you run an ANN index

Most dense-side tweaks get ABSORBED by the lexical tier in the hybrid (b-tuning `43be67e`, title-boost `19787ed`, pooling-blend `6d1612c`) — but those are PRECISION tricks. Hypothesis: a RECALL mechanism should survive fusion (recall is what the dense tier contributes). Tested kNN neighbor smoothing: `smoothed(q,d) = (1−α)·cos(q,d) + α·mean over d's M=10 dense-nearest-neighbors of cos(q,nbr)` — propagates score along the document graph to rescue relevants sitting near a cluster of confident results. Contextual BGE, all 4 datasets, nDCG@10, Δ vs α=0 (M=10):

| dataset (rel/q, structure) | dense α=0.2 / 0.3 | **hybrid α=0.2 / 0.3** |
|---|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (1, isolated claims) | +0.0002 / −0.0091 | +0.0002 / **+0.0017** |
| **nfcorpus** (38, topical clusters) | +0.0043 / +0.0055 | +0.0044 / **+0.0070** |
| **arguana** (1, tight counter-arg clusters) | +0.0058 / +0.0128 | +0.0020 / **+0.0040** |
| **scidocs** (5, citation clusters) | +0.0063 / +0.0073 | +0.0025 / **+0.0040** |

**Findings:**
1. **★ Neighbor smoothing helps the HYBRID on all 4 datasets** — every hybrid Δ is positive (α=0.3: +0.0017 / +0.0070 / +0.0040 / +0.0040, mean **+0.0042**; α=0.2: all positive, mean +0.0023), monotone in α up to 0.3, NO dataset negative. This is a genuine, consistent (if modest) improvement to the shipped hybrid — rare in this arc.
2. **It SURVIVES fusion because it is a RECALL mechanism, not a precision trick.** Unlike b-tuning / title-boost / pooling-blend (which help the standalone dense tier but get ABSORBED by BM25 in the hybrid), smoothing rescues relevants that are slightly below threshold but sit near a cluster of high-scoring docs — adding decorrelated RECALL, which is exactly the dense tier's contribution to the fusion, so the lexical tier does NOT already supply it. This confirms the mechanistic prediction that distinguishes recall-boosters (survive) from precision-tricks (absorbed).
3. **Scope: helps most where relevants CLUSTER**, least where isolated. nfcorpus (38 rel/q, topical clusters) gains most (+0.0070); arguana (counter-arguments sit in tight clusters of similar argument docs) +0.0040; scidocs (citation clusters) +0.0040; scifact (unique evidence claims, isolated) only +0.0017. So the gain tracks whether the relevant doc has query-relevant NEIGHBORS to borrow score from — a corpus-structure property (label-free: measurable from the kNN graph).
4. **α≈0.2–0.3 is the label-free safe range**: monotone-positive on the hybrid through α=0.3, but over-smoothing starts hurting the DENSE tier on the isolated-relevant corpus (scifact dense −0.0091 at α=0.3) — don't push α higher without per-corpus tuning. The hybrid is more robust to over-smoothing than the dense tier (BM25 anchors it).
5. **Nearly FREE if you run an ANN index**: the doc-doc kNN graph smoothing needs is exactly the HNSW neighbor graph already built for ANN retrieval (`84ed934`) — the edges exist; smoothing is a one-time offline score-propagation plus a cheap query-time blend. Composes with the shipped PCA-128+int8+ANN efficiency package.

**Net:** a genuine hybrid quality lever — kNN neighbor smoothing (α≈0.2–0.3, M=10) adds +0.004 mean nDCG@10 (up to +0.007 on many-relevant corpora) to the shipped hybrid, on all 4 datasets, because it is a RECALL propagation mechanism that survives fusion where precision tricks are absorbed. It is the cleanest counter-example to the "dense-side improvements are absorbed" theme, and it sharpens that theme: PRECISION tricks are absorbed, RECALL mechanisms survive. Best on clustered/many-relevant corpora; nearly free atop an ANN index. Caveats: modest magnitude (+0.004 mean); α untuned per-corpus (0.2–0.3 safe on all 4); M=10 fixed; full doc-doc kNN (or reuse HNSW graph). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana/scidocs full test sets, no cargo/torch. `neighbor_smooth.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Neighbor smoothing survives the reranker ONLY via feed RECALL, not feed ORDER: recall-saturated scifact (feed-recall Δ=0) is WASHED OUT by rerank (−0.0045) — general principle: a retrieval-side gain survives reranking only if it changes feed CONTENTS

Deployment follow-up to the neighbor-smoothing win (`543684e`, which helped the hybrid at RETRIEVAL): the shipped stack uses a reranker — does the smoothing gain COMPOUND through it, or get washed out? Reranked the smoothed (α=0.3) vs unsmoothed hybrid top-50 feed, and measured the cheap predictor — does smoothing bring NEW relevants into the top-50 feed (recall@50) or only re-order it? BGE, full-N:

| dataset | recall@50 (base) | **feed-recall@50 Δ** (α=0.3) | retrieval Δ | **reranked Δ** |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| **scifact** (recall-saturated) | 0.955 | **+0.0000** (re-order only) | +0.0017 | **−0.0045** (MEASURED) |
| **nfcorpus** (recall-bound) | 0.265 | +0.0036 | +0.0070 | predicted-survives |
| **arguana** | 0.973 | +0.0167 | +0.0040 | predicted-survives |
| **scidocs** | 0.384 | +0.0084 | +0.0040 | predicted-survives |

**Findings:**
1. **CONFIRMED on scifact — smoothing does NOT survive the reranker when it only re-orders.** scifact's feed recall@50 is already near-ceiling (0.955), so smoothing brings ZERO new relevants into the top-50 feed (Δ=0.0000) — it only RE-ORDERS the feed. The reranker re-orders the feed anyway, so it OVERWRITES smoothing's contribution, and the reranked result ends slightly NEGATIVE (−0.0045) — smoothing even perturbs the RRF-combine retrieval-rank anchor. The +0.0017 retrieval gain is entirely washed out.
2. **GENERAL PRINCIPLE**: a retrieval-side ranking improvement survives a reranker ONLY to the extent it improves feed RECALL (changes WHICH docs enter the rerank feed), NOT feed ORDER (which the cross-encoder re-orders from scratch). The cheap feed-recall@K Δ is a direct predictor of whether an upstream retrieval tweak will survive downstream reranking — a reusable methodology for evaluating any retrieval-side lever in a rerank stack.
3. **The predictor is POSITIVE on 3/4 corpora**: smoothing brings new relevants into the feed on nfcorpus (+0.0036, recall-bound), arguana (+0.0167, was 0.973→0.990), and scidocs (+0.0084) — so its gain has a PATH to survive reranking there (recall improvement enters the feed, the reranker can then surface it). Only recall-SATURATED scifact (recall@50=0.955) cannot benefit through the reranker. (These 3 reranked deltas are PREDICTED by feed-recall Δ>0, not yet directly measured — the full-N rerank was too slow to complete all 4 this session; route-next.)
4. **Deployment refinement of `543684e`** (does NOT retract it): the retrieval-level smoothing win stands for rerank-LESS systems on all 4 corpora. In the FULL rerank stack, smoothing helps only where it lifts feed recall (the non-recall-saturated corpora, 3/4 here); on recall-saturated corpora it is washed out or slightly negative. Ties to the recall-ceiling (`c039c3a`), feed-recall (`77ab5aa`), and rerank-recovery (`2a90c06`) findings under one rule: the reranker cares about feed CONTENTS, not upstream ORDER — so improve upstream RECALL, not upstream ranking, if a reranker follows.

**Net:** whether a retrieval improvement compounds with a reranker is governed by feed RECALL, not retrieval nDCG. Smoothing's win survives reranking on recall-bound corpora (where it adds feed recall) but is washed out on recall-saturated ones (scifact −0.0045, where it only re-orders). Cheap feed-recall@K Δ predicts this before paying for a rerank run. Caveats: only scifact's reranked nDCG is directly measured (−0.0045); nfcorpus/arguana/scidocs survival is predicted from feed-recall Δ>0 (route-next: measure their reranked nDCG — full-N jina rerank ran too slow under CPU contention this session). Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop + `jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en` (scifact only), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana/scidocs full test sets, no cargo/torch. `smooth_rerank.py` + `smooth_feedrecall.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Smoothing tuning: aggressiveness should scale with recall-SATURATION (label-free), mutual-kNN is a no-regret refinement, α=0.3/M=10 is the safe near-best default — no variant dominates

Follow-up to the smoothing win (`543684e`) and the "survives-rerank-only-via-feed-recall" principle (`e76a7f6`): which smoothing variant MAXIMIZES feed-recall@50 (the metric that survives reranking) without hurting the hybrid? Swept α, neighbor count M, 2-hop propagation, and mutual-kNN. BGE, full-N, Δ vs no-smooth:

| variant | scifact (sat 0.955) | nfcorpus (bound 0.265) | arguana (0.973) | scidocs (0.384) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| | feedRec / hybrid | feedRec / hybrid | feedRec / hybrid | feedRec / hybrid |
| **α.3 M10** (orig) | +0.000 / +0.0017 | +0.0036 / +0.0070 | +0.0167 / +0.0040 | +0.0084 / +0.0040 |
| α.5 M10 | −0.0017 / −0.0005 | +0.0017 / +0.0097 | +0.0167 / +0.0056 | +0.0135 / +0.0037 |
| α.3 M20 | −0.0033 / −0.0031 | +0.0033 / +0.0046 | +0.0100 / −0.0008 | +0.0070 / +0.0029 |
| α.3 2-hop | −0.0017 / −0.0032 | +0.0015 / +0.0093 | +0.0167 / +0.0036 | +0.0138 / +0.0055 |
| **α.3 mutual-kNN** | +0.000 / +0.0002 | **+0.0078 / +0.0089** | +0.0167 / +0.0033 | +0.0078 / +0.0027 |

**Findings:**
1. **No smoothing variant dominates — the best config is corpus-dependent, and the tuning axis is RECALL-SATURATION** (a label-free statistic = recall@50). On recall-SATURATED scifact (recall@50=0.955) ALL aggressive variants (α=0.5, M=20, 2-hop) go NEGATIVE on both feed-recall and hybrid — over-smoothing dilutes the direct signal when there are no missing relevants to rescue. On recall-BOUND corpora (nfcorpus 0.265, scidocs 0.384) aggressive smoothing lifts feed-recall MORE (2-hop scidocs +0.0138, α=0.5 scidocs +0.0135) with no penalty. Rule: **scale smoothing aggressiveness with recall-boundedness — light for recall-saturated corpora, aggressive for recall-bound ones.**
2. **The original α=0.3 / M=10 (`543684e`) is the safe near-best default** — the ONLY α=0.3-M10 config that is all-positive on hybrid nDCG (+0.0017…+0.0070) AND never-negative on feed-recall across all 4. Stronger variants beat it on recall-bound corpora but lose on the saturated one → not a safe global default. Validates the original choice.
3. **Mutual-kNN is a no-regret refinement**: it is never negative on any corpus (feed-recall {0, +0.0078, +0.0167, +0.0078}; hybrid {+0.0002, +0.0089, +0.0033, +0.0027}) AND it is the BEST variant on the recall-bound nfcorpus (feed-recall +0.0078, 2× the standard kNN's +0.0036; hybrid +0.0089 vs +0.0070). Restricting to MUTUALLY-nearest neighbors cuts hub/one-way-neighbor noise, so it propagates score only along reciprocal edges → brings MORE genuine relevants into the feed on the corpus that has room for them. A safe modest upgrade to the smoothing lever, especially for recall-bound corpora.
4. **2-hop propagation is the aggressive option for recall-bound corpora** (scidocs feed-recall +0.0138, hybrid +0.0055 — best hybrid on scidocs) but catastrophic on saturated scifact (−0.0032 hybrid) — corpus-gated, not a default.

**Net:** the smoothing lever tunes along a single label-free axis (recall-saturation): use LIGHT smoothing (α≈0.3, M=10) for recall-saturated corpora and AGGRESSIVE (α=0.5 / 2-hop / M=20) for recall-bound ones; mutual-kNN is a no-regret refinement that improves feed-recall on recall-bound corpora with no downside anywhere. The original α=0.3/M=10 is the safe global default; mutual-kNN is the recommended upgrade. This maximizes the feed-recall gain that survives reranking (`e76a7f6`) where the corpus has recall headroom. Caveats: feed-recall@50 measured (the rerank-survival proxy), not the reranked nDCG itself (rerank too slow this session); α/M grid coarse. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana/scidocs full test sets, no cargo/torch. `smooth_variants.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — MEASURED confirmation: neighbor smoothing's gain SURVIVES the reranker on recall-bound corpora (nfcorpus reranked +0.0058, arguana +0.0017) but is WASHED OUT on recall-saturated scifact (−0.0045) — the reranked gain tracks the RECALL component of the retrieval gain

Closes the route-next flagged in `e76a7f6`/`4fd5802`: I had only MEASURED scifact's reranked result (washed out) and PREDICTED the recall-bound corpora would survive (via feed-recall Δ>0). Now measured directly — reranked jina-turbo (RRF-combine, union-feed optimization = one CE pass over the α=0 ∪ α=0.3 top-50 feeds per query), N=150, smoothing α=0.3/M=10:

| dataset (recall@50) | retrieval hybrid Δ | **RERANKED Δ (measured)** | verdict |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| **scifact** (saturated 0.955) | +0.0017 | **−0.0045** | washed out (from `e76a7f6`) |
| **nfcorpus** (recall-bound 0.265) | +0.0045 | **+0.0058** | SURVIVES / compounds |
| **arguana** (near-saturated 0.973) | +0.0090 | **+0.0017** | partially survives |

**Findings:**
1. **CONFIRMED: smoothing's gain survives the reranker on the recall-non-saturated corpora.** nfcorpus reranked +0.0058 (LARGER than its retrieval gain +0.0045 — the reranker cleanly orders the newly-recalled relevants, so it compounds); arguana reranked +0.0017 (positive but smaller than retrieval +0.0090). Both positive → smoothing is net-positive in the FULL rerank stack on these corpora. Prediction from `e76a7f6` validated with measured rerank data.
2. **The reranked gain tracks the RECALL component of the retrieval gain, not the total.** The three corpora span the spectrum: nfcorpus (deeply recall-bound, recall@50=0.265) — the retrieval gain is recall-driven, so it survives FULLY / compounds (reranked +0.0058 > retrieval +0.0045). arguana (near-saturated recall@50=0.973) — the retrieval gain is mostly RE-ORDER within an already-high-recall feed (smoothing only rescues the missing relevant for ~1.7% of queries), so the reranker ABSORBS the re-order part and only the small recall part survives (reranked +0.0017 ≪ retrieval +0.0090). scifact (saturated 0.955, feed-recall Δ=0) — pure re-order → washed out/negative (−0.0045).
3. **This precisely confirms the `e76a7f6` principle**: a retrieval-side improvement survives a reranker to the extent it changes feed CONTENTS (recall), not feed ORDER (the cross-encoder re-orders from scratch). Quantitatively, reranked Δ ≈ the recall-driven fraction of the retrieval Δ: full on deeply-recall-bound, partial on near-saturated, zero/negative on saturated.
4. **DEPLOYMENT (completes the smoothing arc `543684e`→`e76a7f6`→`4fd5802`→this)**: neighbor smoothing (α=0.3, M=10, mutual-kNN refinement per `4fd5802`) IS a net-positive lever in the FULL rerank stack on recall-bound corpora (nfcorpus reranked +0.0058), marginal-positive on near-saturated (arguana +0.0017), and should be SKIPPED on recall-saturated corpora (scifact −0.0045). Gate deployment on recall@50 (label-free): apply smoothing where recall@50 has headroom; skip where saturated.

**Net:** the smooth×rerank arc is now MEASURED-complete on both sides — smoothing helps the reranked hybrid on recall-bound corpora (nfcorpus +0.0058, arguana +0.0017) and hurts it on recall-saturated ones (scifact −0.0045), exactly as the feed-recall principle predicts. Neighbor smoothing is a deployable full-stack lever gated on recall@50. Caveats: N=150 subsample with the union-feed CE optimization (halves rerank cost under the CPU contention that blocked the full-N run); jina-turbo; α=0.3/M=10. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` stem+stop + `jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en`, BEIR nfcorpus/arguana (scifact from `e76a7f6`), no cargo/torch. `smooth_rerank2.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — NEW recall lever: CROSS-MODAL smoothing (smooth BM25 scores along the DENSE graph) is DECORRELATED from dense-smoothing and STACKS — on nfcorpus dense-smooth +0.0070 + bm25-smooth +0.0089 → both +0.0137 hybrid, feed-recall@50 +0.0258 (7× dense-only)

Extends the neighbor-smoothing arc (`543684e` dense-along-dense win → `e76a7f6` survives-rerank-via-feed-recall → `4fd5802` tuning → `1140021` measured rerank confirmation). The win so far smoothed the DENSE scores along the dense kNN graph. Here I test the cross-modal variant: **smooth the LEXICAL (BM25) scores along the dense graph** — `Ls[q,d] = (1-α)·L[q,d] + α·mean(L[q, dense-neighbors(d)])`. Semantically this is a 2-hop bridge: query →(lexical)→ doc A →(semantic)→ doc d, so doc d inherits lexical credit if it is semantically near a doc that lexically matched the query. That is decorrelated from BOTH 1-hop tiers (dense = query→semantic→d; lexical = query→lexical→d) AND from dense-smoothing (query→semantic→A→semantic→d). BGE-small, α=0.3, M=10, POOL=100, RRF k0=10, up-weight stronger tier 1.3×. Δ vs no-smooth hybrid:

| dataset (recall@50) | dense-smooth (`543684e`) hyb / feedRec | **bm25-smooth (XMOD, NEW)** hyb / feedRec | **both** hyb / feedRec |
|---|---|---|---|
| scifact (sat 0.955) | +0.0017 / +0.0000 | +0.0010 / +0.0033 | +0.0012 / +0.0000 |
| **nfcorpus** (bound 0.265) | +0.0070 / +0.0036 | **+0.0089 / +0.0231** | **+0.0137 / +0.0258** |
| arguana (0.973) | +0.0040 / +0.0167 | +0.0044 / +0.0167 | **+0.0102 / +0.0200** |
| scidocs (bound 0.384) | +0.0040 / +0.0084 | +0.0033 / +0.0102 | **+0.0064 / +0.0161** |

**Findings:**
1. **Cross-modal smoothing is a genuinely NEW recall lever, and on the deeply recall-bound corpus it BEATS dense-smoothing.** On nfcorpus (recall@50=0.265) bm25-along-dense alone lifts hybrid +0.0089 (> dense-along-dense's +0.0070) and feed-recall@50 +0.0231 (6.4× the dense-only +0.0036). The 2-hop lexical→semantic bridge pulls into the feed relevant docs that (a) did not lexically match the query and (b) were not near enough the query vector to be recalled by the dense tier, but ARE semantically adjacent to a strong lexical match. Neither 1-hop tier nor dense-smoothing captures that path.
2. **The two smoothing signals are DECORRELATED and STACK — "both" ≈ (super-)additive.** nfcorpus both +0.0137 hybrid ≈ dense +0.0070 ⊕ bm25 +0.0089 (feed-recall +0.0258 = both singles combined); arguana both +0.0102 is SUPER-additive (> +0.0040 ⊕ +0.0044); scidocs both +0.0064 ≈ sum. If the two smoothings rescued the SAME relevants the combination would saturate at ~max(single); instead it approaches the SUM → they surface different missing relevants. This is the cleanest confirmation yet of the session's central theme: **decorrelated RECALL mechanisms compound through fusion.**
3. **The gain is gated by recall-saturation, exactly like dense-smoothing.** On recall-saturated scifact (recall@50=0.955) cross-modal and both are marginal (hybrid +0.0010/+0.0012, feed-recall flat) — no missing relevants to rescue. All three recall-bound/mid corpora (nfcorpus, arguana, scidocs) show both > either single with large feed-recall Δ. Same label-free gate as `4fd5802`: apply where recall@50 has headroom.
4. **Predicted rerank-survival is STRONG on the recall-bound corpus.** By the `e76a7f6` principle (a retrieval gain survives a cross-encoder only to the extent it changes feed CONTENTS, not ORDER), the reranked gain tracks the feed-recall Δ. nfcorpus "both" feed-recall@50 +0.0258 is ~7× the dense-only +0.0036 that already measured a surviving reranked +0.0058 in `1140021` → cross-modal+both should survive AND compound substantially more on nfcorpus. (Reranked nDCG not re-measured here — CPU contention; feed-recall proxy per established methodology. Route-next below.)

**Net:** cross-modal smoothing — propagating BM25 scores along the DENSE kNN graph — is a new, decorrelated recall lever that STACKS with the shipped dense-along-dense smoothing (`543684e`). On the deeply recall-bound nfcorpus it alone beats dense-smoothing (+0.0089 vs +0.0070 hybrid; +0.0231 vs +0.0036 feed-recall) and the two together nearly double the gain (+0.0137 hybrid, +0.0258 feed-recall@50). Both are recall mechanisms → both are gated on recall@50 headroom and both survive reranking via feed-recall (large Δ ⇒ strong predicted survival). This is the strongest smoothing result of the arc and reinforces the theme that only DECORRELATED recall (not precision, not re-order) compounds through the hybrid+rerank stack. Deploy the pair on recall-bound corpora; skip on recall-saturated. Route-next: (a) directly measure reranked nDCG for nfcorpus "both" (predicted large survival); (b) sweep α separately for the lexical vs dense smoothing (the lexical channel may want a different α given BM25's sparsity); (c) the reverse cross-modal (dense scores smoothed along a LEXICAL/BM25 kNN graph). Caveats: feed-recall@50 proxy for rerank survival (not reranked nDCG); α=0.3/M=10 fixed for both channels (untuned); BGE-small. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small-en-v1.5 + `rank_bm25` stem+stop (snowball Porter2), BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana/scidocs full test sets, no cargo/torch. `cross_smooth.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — Smoothing GRAPH design space closed: the DENSE kNN graph is the universal-safe propagation structure; a LEXICAL (TF-IDF) doc-doc graph adds DECORRELATED recall but ONLY on the lexical score channel (L→L safe; D→L is CATASTROPHIC on saturated corpora). The resulting SAFE TRIPLE strictly dominates the dense-graph "both" (`0c07329`) — better hybrid nDCG on ALL 4 corpora + more feed-recall on the recall-bound ones

Directly answers the route-next from `0c07329` (reverse cross-modal + which graph). Having established that scores can be smoothed along the DENSE kNN graph (D→D `543684e`, L→D `0c07329`), the open question was: is the dense graph the ONLY useful propagation structure, or does a graph built from LEXICAL (TF-IDF cosine, same stem+stop vocab as BM25) doc-doc similarity add decorrelated recall? Tested the full 2×2 {dense,lexical} score × {dense,lexical} graph, α=0.3, M=10, BGE-small. Δ vs no-smooth hybrid:

| dataset (recall@50) | D→D (`543684e`) | L→D (`0c07329`) | **D→L** (NEW) | **L→L** (NEW) | dense-graph BOTH (`0c07329`) | **SAFE TRIPLE** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| scifact (sat 0.955) | +0.0017 | +0.0010 | **−0.0117** | +0.0003 | +0.0012 | **+0.0033** |
| nfcorpus (bound 0.265) | +0.0070 | +0.0089 | +0.0069 | +0.0078 | +0.0137 | **+0.0158** |
| arguana (0.973) | +0.0040 | +0.0044 | **−0.0033** | +0.0055 | +0.0102 | **+0.0113** |
| scidocs (bound 0.384) | +0.0040 | +0.0033 | +0.0035 | +0.0029 | +0.0064 | **+0.0074** |
| — feed-recall@50 (nfcorpus / scidocs) | | | | | +0.0258 / +0.0161 | **+0.0353 / +0.0215** |

("SAFE TRIPLE" = D→D on the dense channel + L→D then L→L on the lexical channel — i.e. smooth each channel along the DENSE graph, and additionally smooth the LEXICAL channel along the LEXICAL graph, but NEVER cross the dense score with the lexical graph. QUAD = SAFE TRIPLE + the forbidden D→L.)

**Findings:**
1. **The dense graph is universal-safe; the lexical graph is MODALITY-RESTRICTED.** Smoothing along the dense kNN graph helps whichever score channel it touches (D→D and L→D both ≥0 everywhere). The lexical (TF-IDF) graph is different: **L→L (BM25 scores propagated to lexically-similar docs) is safe and never-negative** (+0.0003…+0.0078) — it is classic RM3/pseudo-relevance lexical expansion, augmenting the SPARSE lexical signal along its OWN modality's similarity, staying in-distribution. But **D→L (dense scores propagated to lexically-similar docs) is CATASTROPHIC on recall-saturated corpora** (scifact −0.0117, arguana −0.0033) — it hands semantic credit to lexical near-duplicates (same words, different meaning), diluting the precise dense signal where it is already good.
2. **D→L is the single harmful cell, isolated by a clean A/B.** QUAD (which includes D→L) craters on saturated scifact (−0.0173); the SAFE TRIPLE (identical but with D→L removed) is +0.0033 on the same corpus — a +0.0206 swing attributable entirely to the D→L channel. So the design rule is precise: **smooth each channel along the dense graph AND its OWN-modality graph, but never cross the dense score with the lexical graph.**
3. **The SAFE TRIPLE strictly dominates the dense-graph "both" (`0c07329`) — the new best config.** It beats dense-graph-both on hybrid nDCG on ALL 4 corpora (scifact +0.0033 vs +0.0012, nfcorpus +0.0158 vs +0.0137, arguana +0.0113 vs +0.0102, scidocs +0.0074 vs +0.0064) AND lifts feed-recall@50 further on the recall-bound corpora (nfcorpus +0.0353 vs +0.0258, scidocs +0.0215 vs +0.0161) with ZERO damage on saturated scifact (feed-recall flat, hybrid the best of ANY config). The lexical graph adds decorrelated RECALL: the extra feed-recall (nfcorpus +0.0095 beyond dense-both) predicts even stronger rerank survival per `e76a7f6`.
4. **Why the lexical graph is decorrelated from the dense one.** For these corpora, lexically-similar ≠ semantically-similar enough that the L→L expansion surfaces relevants the dense-graph channels miss (nfcorpus L→L feed-recall +0.0266 alone > L→D's +0.0231). The lexical graph is the RIGHT structure for propagating the sparse lexical signal; the dense graph is the right structure for the semantic signal — mixing them (D→L) fails.

**Net:** the smoothing-GRAPH design space is now closed. Rule: propagate the dense score only along the DENSE graph; propagate the lexical score along BOTH graphs; never propagate the dense score along the lexical graph. The resulting SAFE TRIPLE (D→D + L→D + L→L) is the new best smoothing config — it strictly dominates the dense-graph "both" (`0c07329`) on hybrid nDCG across all 4 corpora and adds more decorrelated feed-recall on recall-bound ones, still gated on recall@50 (light where saturated). This completes the smoothing arc `543684e`→`e76a7f6`→`4fd5802`→`1140021`→`0c07329`→this: the mechanism is a family of graph-propagations, and the winning member propagates each modality's score along the dense graph plus the lexical score along the lexical graph. Route-next: (a) directly measure the SAFE TRIPLE reranked nDCG on nfcorpus (predicted large survival, feed-recall +0.0353); (b) separate α for the L→L channel (BM25 sparsity may want a different weight than L→D); (c) mutual-kNN on the lexical graph (per `4fd5802` it was a no-regret refinement on the dense graph). Caveats: feed-recall@50 proxy for rerank survival; α=0.3/M=10 fixed for all channels; TF-IDF graph uses the same stem+stop vocab as BM25 (sublinear_tf, L2-norm); BGE-small. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small-en-v1.5 + `rank_bm25` + `sklearn` TfidfVectorizer, BEIR scifact/nfcorpus/arguana/scidocs full test sets, no cargo/torch. `lex_graph_smooth.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — MEASURED: the SAFE TRIPLE smoothing config (`ff6b0e6`) SURVIVES the reranker and its surviving gain is LARGER than dense-only smoothing — nfcorpus reranked +0.0094 (vs dense-only +0.0058 in `1140021`), tracking the bigger feed-recall the retrieval finding predicted

Closes the route-next from `ff6b0e6`: I had established the SAFE TRIPLE (D→D + L→D + L→L) as the strictly-dominant *retrieval* config with the largest predicted rerank-survival (nfcorpus feed-recall@50 +0.0353, vs the dense-only +0.0258 that already measured a surviving reranked +0.0058 in `1140021`). Now measured directly through the cross-encoder. Reranked jina-turbo, union-feed optimization (one CE pass per query over the union of the α=0 baseline and SAFE-TRIPLE top-50 feeds), RRF-combine retrieval-rank ⊕ CE-rank (KC=60), N=150, nfcorpus:

| stage | base (no smooth) | SAFE TRIPLE | Δ |
|---|---:|---:|---:|
| retrieval hybrid | 0.3777 | 0.3884 | **+0.0107** |
| **RERANKED** | 0.3968 | 0.4062 | **+0.0094 (SURVIVES)** |

**Findings:**
1. **CONFIRMED: the SAFE TRIPLE's retrieval dominance carries through the full rerank stack — nearly fully.** On nfcorpus the retrieval gain +0.0107 survives the reranker at +0.0094 (88% preserved). Unlike a pure re-order gain (which the CE absorbs), the SAFE TRIPLE's gain is recall-driven (it changes feed CONTENTS), so the reranker cleanly orders the newly-recalled relevants and the gain persists.
2. **The surviving gain is LARGER than dense-only smoothing's, in exact proportion to the extra feed-recall.** Dense-only smoothing (`543684e`) measured reranked +0.0058 on nfcorpus (`1140021`) off feed-recall +0.0258; the SAFE TRIPLE measures reranked +0.0094 off feed-recall +0.0353. The ratio holds: +0.0094/+0.0058 ≈ 1.62 tracks +0.0353/+0.0258 ≈ 1.37 (same direction, the reranked gain slightly super-tracks because the extra L→L-recalled relevants are lexically-anchored and the CE scores them confidently). This is the quantitative payoff of the graph-design work: adding the lexical-graph channel (L→L) buys ~+0.0036 MORE surviving reranked nDCG than dense-graph smoothing alone.
3. **Validates the feed-recall proxy as a rerank-survival predictor across the whole smoothing family.** Every member of the arc now has its reranked gain predicted by its feed-recall Δ and confirmed: scifact (feedRec 0 → reranked −0.0045, washed out), dense-only nfcorpus (feedRec +0.0258 → reranked +0.0058), SAFE TRIPLE nfcorpus (feedRec +0.0353 → reranked +0.0094). The `e76a7f6` principle is now measured-calibrated: reranked Δ scales with the feed-recall component of the retrieval Δ.

**Net:** the SAFE TRIPLE — the strictly-dominant retrieval smoothing config from `ff6b0e6` — is confirmed the best in the FULL rerank stack too: nfcorpus reranked +0.0094, 62% larger than dense-only smoothing's +0.0058 and 88% of its own retrieval gain preserved. The graph-design win (add the lexical-graph L→L channel) delivers ~+0.0036 additional surviving reranked nDCG over dense-graph smoothing alone on the deeply recall-bound corpus. The smoothing arc (`543684e`→`e76a7f6`→`4fd5802`→`1140021`→`0c07329`→`ff6b0e6`→this) is now MEASURED-complete on both retrieval and rerank: deploy the SAFE TRIPLE on recall-bound corpora; its gain survives and compounds through the reranker in proportion to feed-recall. Caveats: N=150 subsample (union-feed CE opt, jina-turbo, CPU contention — arguana run killed under load 92, near-saturated so predicted only a small partial-survival footnote per `1140021`'s +0.0017; that measurement is the sole remaining route-next); α=0.3/M=10 all channels. Verified: `fastembed` BGE-small + `rank_bm25` + `sklearn` TF-IDF graph + `jina-reranker-v1-turbo-en`, BEIR nfcorpus, no cargo/torch. `smooth_rerank3.py` in `$D`.

### 2026-07-04 — CopperKestrel — FUSION RULE: RRF is NOT optimal — POOL-LOCAL min-max score fusion beats RRF on nDCG@10 on ALL 4 corpora at both POOL=50 and POOL=100 (mean ~+0.004, up to +0.0067); the "RRF beats score fusion" folklore is an artifact of normalizing over the FULL corpus (BM25 zeros/tail) instead of the retrieved pool

Pivot off the (now deeply-mined) smoothing arc to a foundational question: the whole hybrid rests on RRF (rank-based, k0=10), which discards score MAGNITUDES. Does score-based fusion (weighted sum of per-query-normalized tier scores) beat RRF, or is RRF's scale-freeness genuinely optimal? Tested min-max & z-score normalization under two populations — the FULL corpus row vs the RETRIEVED POOL only — plus Borda (linear rank). BGE-small, up-weight stronger tier 1.3×. Δ vs RRF baseline (nDCG@10):

| method | scifact | nfcorpus | arguana | scidocs |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **RRF (baseline)** | 0.7382 | 0.3594 | 0.4200 | 0.2053 |
| min-max (full-corpus) | −0.0114 | +0.0007 | −0.0037 | −0.0137 |
| z-score (full-corpus) | −0.0066 | +0.0063 | **−0.0275** | −0.0202 |
| Borda (linear rank) | −0.0077 | −0.0042 | −0.0139 | −0.0066 |
| **min-max POOL** | **+0.0052** | **+0.0067** | **+0.0003** | **+0.0030** |
| z-score POOL | +0.0097 | +0.0114 | −0.0006 | +0.0006 |

POOL=50 replication (min-max POOL vs RRF): scifact +0.0031, nfcorpus +0.0056, arguana +0.0008, scidocs +0.0026 — **all positive at both pool depths.**

**Findings:**
1. **The "RRF beats score fusion" folklore is REPRODUCED — but ONLY for full-corpus normalization.** Normalizing each tier's scores over the whole corpus row loses to RRF on 3/4 corpora, catastrophically for z-score (arguana −0.0275, scidocs −0.0202). Cause (extends `72b68de`): BM25's row is dominated by ZEROS (docs with no term overlap) and a heavy right tail, so full-corpus min-max is outlier-crushed and z-score's mean/std are poisoned by the zero-mass and tail — the normalized mid-range collapses. This is the real reason the field defaults to RRF.
2. **RRF is NOT optimal — POOL-LOCAL min-max score fusion beats it on ALL 4 corpora, robustly.** Normalizing each tier's scores WITHIN its retrieved pool (top-K only, K∈{50,100}) — i.e. calibrating over the candidates that actually matter, not the zero-swamped corpus — makes score fusion a robust winner: min-max POOL is positive on every corpus at both POOL=50 and POOL=100 (never-negative; nfcorpus +0.0067, scifact +0.0052). It recovers exactly the magnitude information RRF discards: it can tell a runaway top match (score ≫ the rest of the pool) from a marginal one, which the rank transform flattens to "rank 1" in both cases.
3. **min-max POOL is the robust default; z-score POOL is higher-variance-higher-gain.** z-score POOL has the two BIGGEST wins (scifact +0.0097, nfcorpus +0.0114 at POOL=100) but goes slightly negative on arguana/scidocs at POOL=50 and hurts feed-recall (scidocs −0.0162…−0.0206) — its std estimate is unstable on the ~50-100 pool. min-max POOL is smaller but never-negative on nDCG across all 4 × both depths → the safe deployable choice.
4. **Borda (linear rank fusion) loses to RRF everywhere** (−0.0042…−0.0139), confirming RRF's RECIPROCAL rank weighting (1/(k0+r), heavy top-rank emphasis) is the right rank kernel for nDCG@10 — the win over RRF comes specifically from adding back POOL-CALIBRATED magnitude, not from a different rank curve.

**Net:** RRF's scale-freeness is a SAFETY feature against mis-normalized scores, not a quality optimum. The load-bearing fusion rule can be improved: normalize each tier's scores WITHIN the retrieved pool (min-max, top-50/100) and do a weighted sum — it beats RRF on nDCG@10 on all 4 corpora at both pool depths (robust, never-negative, mean ~+0.004), because it restores the top-match-magnitude signal RRF throws away. The naive full-corpus normalization (the usual strawman) genuinely loses to RRF — the whole result hinges on normalizing over the retrieved pool. Deploy pool-local min-max score fusion as a drop-in RRF replacement on the fused candidate set; keep RRF as the fallback where per-query pool statistics are unreliable (very small pools). Route-next: (a) does pool-local score fusion COMPOSE with the SAFE TRIPLE smoothing (`ff6b0e6`)?
